texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Identifiability is central to the interpretability of deep latent variable models, ensuring parameterisations are uniquely determined by the data-generating distribution. However, it remains underexplored for deep regime-switching time series. We develop a general theoretical framework for multi-lag Regime-Switching Models (RSMs), encompassing Markov Switching Models (MSMs) and Switching Dynamical Systems (SDSs). For MSMs, we formulate the model as a temporally structured finite mixture and prove identifiability of both the number of regimes and the multi-lag transitions in a nonlinear-Gaussian setting. For SDSs, we establish identifiability of the latent variables up to permutation and scaling via temporal structure, which in turn yields conditions for identifiability of regime-dependent latent causal graphs (up to regime/node permutations). Our results hold in a fully unsupervised setting through architectural and noise assumptions that are directly enforceable via neural network design. We complement the theory with a flexible variational estimator that satisfies the assumptions and validate the results on synthetic benchmarks. Across real-world datasets from neuroscience, finance, and climate, identifiability leads to more trustworthy interpretability analysis, which is crucial for scientific discovery.
texts.text: Identifiability is central to the interpretability of deep latent variable models, ensuring parameterisations are uniquely determined by the data-generating distribution. However, it remains underexplored for deep regime-switching time series. We develop a general theoretical framework for multi-lag Regime-Switching Models (RSMs), encompassing Markov Switching Models (MSMs) and Switching Dynamical Systems (SDSs). For MSMs, we formulate the model as a temporally structured finite mixture and prove identifiability of both the number of regimes and the multi-lag transitions in a nonlinear-Gaussian setting. For SDSs, we establish identifiability of the latent variables up to permutation and scaling via temporal structure, which in turn yields conditions for identifiability of regime-dependent latent causal graphs (up to regime/node permutations). Our results hold in a fully unsupervised setting through architectural and noise assumptions that are directly enforceable via neural network design. We complement the theory with a flexible variational estimator that satisfies the assumptions and validate the results on synthetic benchmarks. Across real-world datasets from neuroscience, finance, and climate, identifiability leads to more trustworthy interpretability analysis, which is crucial for scientific discovery.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1 Introduction
texts.text: 1 Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Identifiability in deep latent variable models has been a long-standing challenge over the recent years. It formalises when parameters are uniquely determined by the data-generating distribution. In practice, exact parameter recovery is often impossible, and instead, one assumes equivalence classes which are determined by the target of interest. For example, Causal Representation Learning (CRL; (Schölkopf et al., 2021)), targets the recovery of the latent causal graph, typically up to permutation of nodes. In Independent Component Analysis (ICA; (Comon, 1994)), the equivalence class targets independent sources up to permutations and scaling, and often the functional form of the mixing function.
texts.text: Identifiability in deep latent variable models has been a long-standing challenge over the recent years. It formalises when parameters are uniquely determined by the data-generating distribution. In practice, exact parameter recovery is often impossible, and instead, one assumes equivalence classes which are determined by the target of interest. For example, Causal Representation Learning (CRL; (Schölkopf et al., 2021)), targets the recovery of the latent causal graph, typically up to permutation of nodes. In Independent Component Analysis (ICA; (Comon, 1994)), the equivalence class targets independent sources up to permutations and scaling, and often the functional form of the mixing function.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: General, nonlinear ICA is ill-posed without additional structure (Hyvärinen and Pajunen, 1999). Recent advances establish identifiability by combining deep generative models with auxiliary information (Khemakhem et al., 2020), or finite mixture models with piecewise linear mixing (Kivva et al., 2022). Other advances exploit temporal structure (Hyvarinen and Morioka, 2017; Hyvarinen et al., 2019; Hälvä and Hyvarinen, 2020; Hälvä et al., 2021), or latent causal processes (Yao et al., 2022a,b; Lippe et al., 2023b,a).
texts.text: General, nonlinear ICA is ill-posed without additional structure (Hyvärinen and Pajunen, 1999). Recent advances establish identifiability by combining deep generative models with auxiliary information (Khemakhem et al., 2020), or finite mixture models with piecewise linear mixing (Kivva et al., 2022). Other advances exploit temporal structure (Hyvarinen and Morioka, 2017; Hyvarinen et al., 2019; Hälvä and Hyvarinen, 2020; Hälvä et al., 2021), or latent causal processes (Yao et al., 2022a,b; Lippe et al., 2023b,a).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This paper focuses on identifiability for Regime-Switching Models (RSMs), including Markov Switching Models (MSMs) and Switching Dynamical Systems (SDSs), from the perspective of deep sequential generative models. RSMs describe time series whose behaviour depends on a discrete latent regime (state, or switch) that changes over time. They form a subset of State-Space Models (SSMs), a well-established class of sequence models (Lindgren, 1978; Poritz, 1982; Hamilton, 1989). With the increasing popularity of sequential latent variable models (Chung et al., 2015; Li and Mandt, 2018; Babaeizadeh et al., 2018; Saxena et al., 2021), SSMs have been incorporated as latent dynamical priors (Johnson et al., 2016; Linderman et al., 2016, 2017; Fraccaro et al., 2017; Dong et al., 2020; Ansari et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2023; Geadah et al., 2024). However, these works mainly focus on designing flexible priors and stable training schemes (Dong et al., 2020; Ansari et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2023; Zhao and Linderman, 2023).
texts.text: This paper focuses on identifiability for Regime-Switching Models (RSMs), including Markov Switching Models (MSMs) and Switching Dynamical Systems (SDSs), from the perspective of deep sequential generative models. RSMs describe time series whose behaviour depends on a discrete latent regime (state, or switch) that changes over time. They form a subset of State-Space Models (SSMs), a well-established class of sequence models (Lindgren, 1978; Poritz, 1982; Hamilton, 1989). With the increasing popularity of sequential latent variable models (Chung et al., 2015; Li and Mandt, 2018; Babaeizadeh et al., 2018; Saxena et al., 2021), SSMs have been incorporated as latent dynamical priors (Johnson et al., 2016; Linderman et al., 2016, 2017; Fraccaro et al., 2017; Dong et al., 2020; Ansari et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2023; Geadah et al., 2024). However, these works mainly focus on designing flexible priors and stable training schemes (Dong et al., 2020; Ansari et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2023; Zhao and Linderman, 2023).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To address these gaps, we develop a unified theoretical framework that establishes identifiability for RSMs with multi-lag dependencies. First, we show that multi-lag MSMs can be framed as finite mixture models with temporally-structured components, and prove identifiability of the number of regimes and the multi-lag transition functions in a nonlinear Gaussian setting. The analysis extends classical finite-mixture identifiability (Yakowitz and Spragins, 1968) to the multi-lag case, which is nontrivial due to lagged dependencies. Second, for SDSs, we prove identifiability of the latent variables up to permutation and scaling by extending Kivva et al. (2022) to the temporal setting. This yields identifiability of the regime-dependent latent causal graphs up to permutations of regimes and nodes. Our results hold in a fully unsupervised setting by imposing restrictions on the latent noise distributions and the functional class of the generative model. In contrast, other works that handle regime-switching require auxiliary information (Yao et al., 2022a,b; Lippe et al., 2023a) or stationarity (Hyvarinen and Morioka, 2017) in the temporal correlations (Hälvä et al., 2021). Recently, similar results with flexible assumptions have been established (Song et al., 2023, 2024; Balsells-Rodas et al., 2025).
texts.text: To address these gaps, we develop a unified theoretical framework that establishes identifiability for RSMs with multi-lag dependencies. First, we show that multi-lag MSMs can be framed as finite mixture models with temporally-structured components, and prove identifiability of the number of regimes and the multi-lag transition functions in a nonlinear Gaussian setting. The analysis extends classical finite-mixture identifiability (Yakowitz and Spragins, 1968) to the multi-lag case, which is nontrivial due to lagged dependencies. Second, for SDSs, we prove identifiability of the latent variables up to permutation and scaling by extending Kivva et al. (2022) to the temporal setting. This yields identifiability of the regime-dependent latent causal graphs up to permutations of regimes and nodes. Our results hold in a fully unsupervised setting by imposing restrictions on the latent noise distributions and the functional class of the generative model. In contrast, other works that handle regime-switching require auxiliary information (Yao et al., 2022a,b; Lippe et al., 2023a) or stationarity (Hyvarinen and Morioka, 2017) in the temporal correlations (Hälvä et al., 2021). Recently, similar results with flexible assumptions have been established (Song et al., 2023, 2024; Balsells-Rodas et al., 2025).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The rest of the paper is organised as follows. 1 Section 2 discusses related work and positions our contributions. Section 3 reviews finite mixtures and latent variable models. Section 4, presents our main results: (i) parametric MSMs with nonlinear Gaussian transitions (nonparametric analysis is deferred to Appendix A), (ii) identifiability of SDS latents up to permutation and scaling, and (iii) identifiability of regime-dependent causal graphs up to regime/node permutation. Section 5 presents a flexible estimation approach which follows the assumptions for identifiability, and builds on Dong et al. (2020); Ansari et al. (2021). Section 6 validates our theoretical framework on synthetic data with baselines and ablations. Finally, Section 7, presents results on brain activity, financial time series, and climate data, illustrating interpretability gains enabled by identifiability.
texts.text: The rest of the paper is organised as follows. 1 Section 2 discusses related work and positions our contributions. Section 3 reviews finite mixtures and latent variable models. Section 4, presents our main results: (i) parametric MSMs with nonlinear Gaussian transitions (nonparametric analysis is deferred to Appendix A), (ii) identifiability of SDS latents up to permutation and scaling, and (iii) identifiability of regime-dependent causal graphs up to regime/node permutation. Section 5 presents a flexible estimation approach which follows the assumptions for identifiability, and builds on Dong et al. (2020); Ansari et al. (2021). Section 6 validates our theoretical framework on synthetic data with baselines and ablations. Finally, Section 7, presents results on brain activity, financial time series, and climate data, illustrating interpretability gains enabled by identifiability.

texts.label: footnote
texts.orig: 1. This paper extends the conference version Balsells-Rodas et al. (2024) significantly by: (i) providing a more intuitive view of Markov Switching Models as finite mixtures and extending identifiability to higher temporal dependencies (with substantial new proofs and intuitions in the main text), (ii) going beyond affine identifiability of the latent variables for SDSs to establish permutation/scaling identifiability and causal structure identifiability up to node/regime permutation, and (iii) adding new real-world applications.
texts.text: 1. This paper extends the conference version Balsells-Rodas et al. (2024) significantly by: (i) providing a more intuitive view of Markov Switching Models as finite mixtures and extending identifiability to higher temporal dependencies (with substantial new proofs and intuitions in the main text), (ii) going beyond affine identifiability of the latent variables for SDSs to establish permutation/scaling identifiability and causal structure identifiability up to node/regime permutation, and (iii) adding new real-world applications.

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: Identifiability of Regime-Switching Models
texts.text: Identifiability of Regime-Switching Models

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Why identifiability matters in practice. Applications across neuroscience (KarniolTambour et al., 2024), climate (Saggioro et al., 2020), human motion (Dong et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2023), and animal behaviour (Ansari et al., 2021) demonstrate the promise of deep regime-switching models. However, without identifiability, interpretations of the learned latents (e.g., regimes, causal structures) may be spurious, as distinct or even contradictory parametrisations can achieve the same likelihood, thus invalidating scientific discovery. More broadly, identifiability prevents arbitrary latent reparametrisations (Locatello et al., 2019) and supports valid causal claims (Schölkopf et al., 2021). Our theoretical framework provides flexible conditions under which learned regimes, latents, and graphs are uniquely determined up to acceptable equivalences, and therefore supporting trustworthy interpretations.
texts.text: Why identifiability matters in practice. Applications across neuroscience (KarniolTambour et al., 2024), climate (Saggioro et al., 2020), human motion (Dong et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2023), and animal behaviour (Ansari et al., 2021) demonstrate the promise of deep regime-switching models. However, without identifiability, interpretations of the learned latents (e.g., regimes, causal structures) may be spurious, as distinct or even contradictory parametrisations can achieve the same likelihood, thus invalidating scientific discovery. More broadly, identifiability prevents arbitrary latent reparametrisations (Locatello et al., 2019) and supports valid causal claims (Schölkopf et al., 2021). Our theoretical framework provides flexible conditions under which learned regimes, latents, and graphs are uniquely determined up to acceptable equivalences, and therefore supporting trustworthy interpretations.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2 Related Work
texts.text: 2 Related Work

texts.label: text
texts.orig: MSMs (or autoregressive hidden Markov models) were first introduced for applications to speech analysis (Poritz, 1982; Ephraim and Roberts, 2005). In econometrics, Hamilton (1989) popularised MSMs for business cycles, with numerous extensions such as MS-GARCH (Haas et al., 2004). For overviews of asymptotic properties and additional applications, see Frühwirth-Schnatter and Frèuhwirth-Schnatter (2006). SDSs extend MSMs with continuous latent states and regime-dependent dynamics. Classic developments include switching Kalman filters (Murphy, 1998) and variational learning with applications to respiration data (Ghahramani and Hinton, 2000). More recently, regime switching has been used as a prior within deep latent-variable models, including SVAEs (Johnson et al., 2016; Zhao and Linderman, 2023), soft-switching Kalman Filters (Fraccaro et al., 2017), and recurrent SDSs (Linderman et al., 2016; Dong et al., 2020) with explicit duration models (Ansari et al., 2021) or graph-based interactions (Liu et al., 2023).
texts.text: MSMs (or autoregressive hidden Markov models) were first introduced for applications to speech analysis (Poritz, 1982; Ephraim and Roberts, 2005). In econometrics, Hamilton (1989) popularised MSMs for business cycles, with numerous extensions such as MS-GARCH (Haas et al., 2004). For overviews of asymptotic properties and additional applications, see Frühwirth-Schnatter and Frèuhwirth-Schnatter (2006). SDSs extend MSMs with continuous latent states and regime-dependent dynamics. Classic developments include switching Kalman filters (Murphy, 1998) and variational learning with applications to respiration data (Ghahramani and Hinton, 2000). More recently, regime switching has been used as a prior within deep latent-variable models, including SVAEs (Johnson et al., 2016; Zhao and Linderman, 2023), soft-switching Kalman Filters (Fraccaro et al., 2017), and recurrent SDSs (Linderman et al., 2016; Dong et al., 2020) with explicit duration models (Ansari et al., 2021) or graph-based interactions (Liu et al., 2023).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Modern identifiability for deep generative models often builds on nonlinear ICA with auxiliary variables or temporal structure. iVAE proves identifiability with conditional priors from auxiliary information (Khemakhem et al., 2020); Kivva et al. (2022) remove this requirement by using mixture priors with piecewise linear mixing. Temporal information can also act as auxiliary information, yielding identifiability via contrastive learning (Hyvarinen and Morioka, 2016; Hyvarinen et al., 2019). In CRL, temporal identifiability has been established under distribution shifts or latent temporal structure (Yao et al., 2022b,a; Lippe et al., 2023b,a).
texts.text: Modern identifiability for deep generative models often builds on nonlinear ICA with auxiliary variables or temporal structure. iVAE proves identifiability with conditional priors from auxiliary information (Khemakhem et al., 2020); Kivva et al. (2022) remove this requirement by using mixture priors with piecewise linear mixing. Temporal information can also act as auxiliary information, yielding identifiability via contrastive learning (Hyvarinen and Morioka, 2016; Hyvarinen et al., 2019). In CRL, temporal identifiability has been established under distribution shifts or latent temporal structure (Yao et al., 2022b,a; Lippe et al., 2023b,a).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Finite-state HMMs enjoy strong identifiability under mild conditions (Allman et al., 2009; Gassiat et al., 2016, 2020). However, these strategies do not translate directly to MSMs due to explicit autoregression in continuous variables. While results exist for certain discrete MSMs (An et al., 2013), a general nonparametric theory for multi-lag MSMs has been missing. Our MSM identifiability results address this gap by extending classical finite-mixture identifiability (Yakowitz and Spragins, 1968) to the multi-lag setting.
texts.text: Finite-state HMMs enjoy strong identifiability under mild conditions (Allman et al., 2009; Gassiat et al., 2016, 2020). However, these strategies do not translate directly to MSMs due to explicit autoregression in continuous variables. While results exist for certain discrete MSMs (An et al., 2013), a general nonparametric theory for multi-lag MSMs has been missing. Our MSM identifiability results address this gap by extending classical finite-mixture identifiability (Yakowitz and Spragins, 1968) to the multi-lag setting.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Several works introduce latent regime structure to relax stationarity or the need for auxiliary information. HM-NICA replaces observed regimes with an identifiable stationary HMM prior (Gassiat et al., 2016); IIA-HMM (Morioka et al., 2021) introduces a recurrent mixing with nonstationary innovations through a stationary HMM (Gassiat et al., 2016). SNICA (Hälvä et al., 2021) considers linear SDSs with weak temporal dependencies (weak stationarity (Hyvarinen and Morioka, 2017)), but does not identify the number of switches or latent transitions. NCTRL (Song et al., 2023) establishes results for SDSs. The strategy follows firs-order MSM results presented in Balsells-Rodas et al. (2024), with Gaussianity imposed at the observation level. CtrlNS (Song et al., 2024) achieves identifiability via mechanism sparsity (Lachapelle et al., 2022) and direct observation-regime dependence, assuming a known number of regimes. Very recent works explicitly target regime-dependent causal discovery: CASTOR (Rahmani and Frossard, 2025) assumes contemporaneous effects and SDCI (Balsells-Rodas et al., 2025) allows flexible state dependencies; both rely on differing causal structures across regimes. Our results are complementary to these methodologies, as multi-lag MSMs can be viewed as a generalisation directly targeting regime-dependent transitions.
texts.text: Several works introduce latent regime structure to relax stationarity or the need for auxiliary information. HM-NICA replaces observed regimes with an identifiable stationary HMM prior (Gassiat et al., 2016); IIA-HMM (Morioka et al., 2021) introduces a recurrent mixing with nonstationary innovations through a stationary HMM (Gassiat et al., 2016). SNICA (Hälvä et al., 2021) considers linear SDSs with weak temporal dependencies (weak stationarity (Hyvarinen and Morioka, 2017)), but does not identify the number of switches or latent transitions. NCTRL (Song et al., 2023) establishes results for SDSs. The strategy follows firs-order MSM results presented in Balsells-Rodas et al. (2024), with Gaussianity imposed at the observation level. CtrlNS (Song et al., 2024) achieves identifiability via mechanism sparsity (Lachapelle et al., 2022) and direct observation-regime dependence, assuming a known number of regimes. Very recent works explicitly target regime-dependent causal discovery: CASTOR (Rahmani and Frossard, 2025) assumes contemporaneous effects and SDCI (Balsells-Rodas et al., 2025) allows flexible state dependencies; both rely on differing causal structures across regimes. Our results are complementary to these methodologies, as multi-lag MSMs can be viewed as a generalisation directly targeting regime-dependent transitions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Positioning. Compared with prior identifiability results for regime-switching and temporal CRL/ICA, our framework offers the following key advantages:
texts.text: Positioning. Compared with prior identifiability results for regime-switching and temporal CRL/ICA, our framework offers the following key advantages:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Multi-Lag MSM theory. To our knowledge, we provide the first general identifiability results for multi-lag MSMs. Our results also generalise beyond standard MSMs (Remark 9).
texts.text: Multi-Lag MSM theory. To our knowledge, we provide the first general identifiability results for multi-lag MSMs. Our results also generalise beyond standard MSMs (Remark 9).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Assumptions at the function level. Related works on temporal CRL (Yao et al., 2022b,a; Song et al., 2023, 2024) and ICA (Morioka et al., 2021; Hälvä et al., 2021) often rely on distributional or tail assumptions that are difficult to verify when designing models. Instead, we directly impose functional constraints that can be enforced via the neural parametrisation, making model specification more controllable.
texts.text: Assumptions at the function level. Related works on temporal CRL (Yao et al., 2022b,a; Song et al., 2023, 2024) and ICA (Morioka et al., 2021; Hälvä et al., 2021) often rely on distributional or tail assumptions that are difficult to verify when designing models. Instead, we directly impose functional constraints that can be enforced via the neural parametrisation, making model specification more controllable.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Unknown number of regimes. Methods such as HMM-ICA (Hälvä and Hyvarinen, 2020) and CtrlNS (Song et al., 2024) assume a known number of regimes. By extending classical finite-mixture theory (Yakowitz and Spragins, 1968), our results do not require knowing the number of regimes. This matters in practice, as we can determine them by model selection (McLachlan and Peel, 2000), which is not possible when the number of regimes is assumed known.
texts.text: Unknown number of regimes. Methods such as HMM-ICA (Hälvä and Hyvarinen, 2020) and CtrlNS (Song et al., 2024) assume a known number of regimes. By extending classical finite-mixture theory (Yakowitz and Spragins, 1968), our results do not require knowing the number of regimes. This matters in practice, as we can determine them by model selection (McLachlan and Peel, 2000), which is not possible when the number of regimes is assumed known.

texts.orig: Definition 1 A family of functions F A (Eq. (1) ) is said to contain linearly independent functions under finite mixtures if for any A 0 ⊂ A such that |A 0 | < + ∞ , the functions in { F a ( z ) | a ∈ A 0 } are linearly independent.
texts.text: Definition 1 A family of functions F A (Eq. (1) ) is said to contain linearly independent functions under finite mixtures if for any A 0 ⊂ A such that |A 0 | < + ∞ , the functions in { F a ( z ) | a ∈ A 0 } are linearly independent.

texts.label: text

texts.orig: Definition 2 The finite mixture family H A is said to be identifiable up to permutations, when for any two finite mixtures H ( z ) = ∑ K i =1 c i F a i ( z ) and ˜ H ( z ) = ∑ ˜ K i =1 ˜ c i F ˜ a i ( z ) , H ( z ) = ˜ H ( z ) for all z ∈ R m , if and only if K = ˜ K and for each 1 ≤ i ≤ K there is some 1 ≤ j ≤ ˜ K such that c i = ˜ c j and F a i ( z ) = F ˜ a j ( z ) for all z ∈ R m .

Specifically, consider a generative model where its latent variables z ∈ R m are drawn from a Gaussian mixture prior with K components ( K < + ∞ ). Then z is transformed via a (noisy) piecewise linear mapping to obtain the observation x ∈ R n , n ≥ m :

(2022) established that if the transformation f is weakly injective (see definition below), the prior distribution p ( z ) is identifiable up to affine transformations 2 from the observations. In Section 4.2 we extend these results to establish identifiability for SDSs using similar proof strategies.

texts.orig: 3.3 Switching Dynamical Systems
texts.text: 3.3 Switching Dynamical Systems

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A Switching Dynamical System (SDS), with an example graphical model illustrated in Figure 1, is a sequential latent variable model with its dynamics governed by both discrete and continuous latent states, s t ∈ { 1 , . . . , K } , z t ∈ R m , respectively. At each time step t , the discrete state s t determines the regime of the dynamical prior that the current continuous latent variable z t should follow, and the observation x t is generated from z t using a (noisy) nonlinear transformation. This gives the following probabilistic model for the states and the observation variables:
texts.text: A Switching Dynamical System (SDS), with an example graphical model illustrated in Figure 1, is a sequential latent variable model with its dynamics governed by both discrete and continuous latent states, s t ∈ { 1 , . . . , K } , z t ∈ R m , respectively. At each time step t , the discrete state s t determines the regime of the dynamical prior that the current continuous latent variable z t should follow, and the observation x t is generated from z t using a (noisy) nonlinear transformation. This gives the following probabilistic model for the states and the observation variables:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The latent dynamic prior p θ ( z 1: T ) considers a Markov Switching Model (MSM) which has also been referred to as autoregressive HMM (Hamilton, 1989; Ephraim and Roberts, 2005). This type of prior uses the latent 'switch' s t to condition the distribution of z t at each time-step, and the conditional dynamic model of z 1: T given s 1: T follows an autoregressive process, where the likelihood can be computed by marginalising s 1: T . For simplicity, we consider s 1: M := s M and p θ ( z 1 , . . . , z M | s M ) as the distribution of the initial M observations, and the MSM with lag M factorises as follows:
texts.text: The latent dynamic prior p θ ( z 1: T ) considers a Markov Switching Model (MSM) which has also been referred to as autoregressive HMM (Hamilton, 1989; Ephraim and Roberts, 2005). This type of prior uses the latent 'switch' s t to condition the distribution of z t at each time-step, and the conditional dynamic model of z 1: T given s 1: T follows an autoregressive process, where the likelihood can be computed by marginalising s 1: T . For simplicity, we consider s 1: M := s M and p θ ( z 1 , . . . , z M | s M ) as the distribution of the initial M observations, and the MSM with lag M factorises as follows:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: p θ ( z 1: T ) = ∑ s M : T p θ ( s M : T ) p θ ( z 1: T | s M : T ) , p θ ( z 1: T | s M : T ) = p θ ( z 1 , . . . , z M | s M ) T ∏ p θ ( z t | z t -1 , . . . , z t -M , s t ) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Note that the structure of the discrete latent state prior p θ ( s M : T ) is not specified, and the identifiability results presented in the next section do not require further assumptions herein. In experiments, we use a first-order Markov process for p θ ( s M : T ) , described by a transition matrix Q ∈ R K × K such that p θ ( s t = j | s t -1 = i ) = Q ij , and an initial distribution p θ ( s M = i ) = π i .
texts.text: Note that the structure of the discrete latent state prior p θ ( s M : T ) is not specified, and the identifiability results presented in the next section do not require further assumptions herein. In experiments, we use a first-order Markov process for p θ ( s M : T ) , described by a transition matrix Q ∈ R K × K such that p θ ( s t = j | s t -1 = i ) = Q ij , and an initial distribution p θ ( s M = i ) = π i .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4 Identifiability Analysis
texts.text: 4 Identifiability Analysis

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section, we establish the identifiability of the SDS (Eq. (5)) presented in Section 3.3 under suitable assumptions. We start by extending finite mixture model identifiability results introduced in Section 3.1 to Markov switching models with finite lag M . Then, we leverage ideas from Kivva et al. (2022) which use finite mixture prior for non-temporal generative models; importantly, this theory relies on the use of identifiable finite mixture priors (up to mixture component permutations).
texts.text: In this section, we establish the identifiability of the SDS (Eq. (5)) presented in Section 3.3 under suitable assumptions. We start by extending finite mixture model identifiability results introduced in Section 3.1 to Markov switching models with finite lag M . Then, we leverage ideas from Kivva et al. (2022) which use finite mixture prior for non-temporal generative models; importantly, this theory relies on the use of identifiable finite mixture priors (up to mixture component permutations).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Identifiability: Formally, identifiability establishes an injective correspondence between probability distributions p θ ( x ) and model parameters θ :
texts.text: Identifiability: Formally, identifiability establishes an injective correspondence between probability distributions p θ ( x ) and model parameters θ :

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Given that our models are parametrised by deep neural networks, explicit identifiability of parameters is not possible due to network symmetries (i.e., different parameters map to the same function). Instead, in this paper we refer to identifiability in terms of their functional form, and model mixture components.
texts.text: Given that our models are parametrised by deep neural networks, explicit identifiability of parameters is not possible due to network symmetries (i.e., different parameters map to the same function). Instead, in this paper we refer to identifiability in terms of their functional form, and model mixture components.

To exemplify, we can consider these families to be infinitely many parametrised functions where each a ∈ A corresponds to a different parameter configuration θ a . We use different calligraphic symbols (e.g., A , B ) because they index functions with different structures and output domains. For example, if the initial distribution family is a Gaussian family, the set A indexes elements in a finite-dimensional parameter space (means and covariances). In contrast, the set B used in the transition family indexes elements in a function space, the output of which defines the parameters of a conditional distribution. We provide details of parametrisations below.

(12) forms a strict subset of conv( P T,M A , B ) , since the mixture components are restricted to finite subsets A 0 ⊂ A and B 0 ⊂ B . On the other hand, mixtures over P T,M A , B allow arbitrary selections from B at each time step, yielding a more expressive model family. This generalisation is crucial for our theoretical analysis below, where we prove identifiability of MSM by establishing linear independence under finite mixtures of P T,M A , B .

(14), (16) respectively.. Then, the MSM is identifiable in terms of Def. 7 under the following assumptions:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. Under the finite mixture model view, it suffices to show that the trajectory family, P T,M A , B , is linearly independent under finite mixtures (Yakowitz and Spragins, 1968). Given that M T (Π M A , P M B ) is a subset of conv( P T,M A , B ) , identifiability of conv( P T,M A , B ) implies the identifiability of M T (Π M A , P M B ) .
texts.text: Under the finite mixture model view, it suffices to show that the trajectory family, P T,M A , B , is linearly independent under finite mixtures (Yakowitz and Spragins, 1968). Given that M T (Π M A , P M B ) is a subset of conv( P T,M A , B ) , identifiability of conv( P T,M A , B ) implies the identifiability of M T (Π M A , P M B ) .

texts.label: list_item

As mentioned, for NNs identifiability applies to the functional form only, since NN weights do not uniquely index NN functions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Nonparametric identifiability. The key to our proof technique is the establishment of step (2) in Proof sketch , which is proved through a new theoretical result: Linear Independence on Product functions with M consecutive Overlapping variables (LIPO-M; Lemma 21), and it can be of independent interest. Unlike Hidden Markov Models (HMMs), in MSMs, z t and z t +1 are not conditionally independent given the discrete states (assuming M = 1 in this example). Therefore, seminal HMM identifiability results (Allman et al., 2009; Gassiat et al., 2016), which exploit conditional independence of z t and z t +1 in HMMs and leverage Kruskal's '3-way arrays' technique (Kruskal, 1977), do not apply to nonparametric MSMs, rendering our development of new theory necessary.
texts.text: Nonparametric identifiability. The key to our proof technique is the establishment of step (2) in Proof sketch , which is proved through a new theoretical result: Linear Independence on Product functions with M consecutive Overlapping variables (LIPO-M; Lemma 21), and it can be of independent interest. Unlike Hidden Markov Models (HMMs), in MSMs, z t and z t +1 are not conditionally independent given the discrete states (assuming M = 1 in this example). Therefore, seminal HMM identifiability results (Allman et al., 2009; Gassiat et al., 2016), which exploit conditional independence of z t and z t +1 in HMMs and leverage Kruskal's '3-way arrays' technique (Kruskal, 1977), do not apply to nonparametric MSMs, rendering our development of new theory necessary.

texts.orig: where the overlapping variable z 1 challenges linear independence under finite mixtures. Given linear independence under finite mixtures on the family Π 1 A (a3 - Lemma 16), the overlapping variable z 1 complicates linear independence under finite mixtures of P 1 B when multiplied with Π 1 A . This issue is resolved by enforcing linear independence under finite mixtures on specific nonzero measure subsets for P 1 B (a4 - Lemma 16), linear dependence implying repeating functions (a5- Lemma 16); and continuity for z 1 (a6- Lemma 16). Figure 3 illustrates the intuition behind this result, where linear independence under finite mixtures of the product family can be achieved as long as linear dependence only happens in zero measure sets. For the case M = 1 the above needs to hold in at least a nonzero measure set of the overlapping variable space.

texts.orig: Linear independence (general case). Our strategy to generalise to any lag M < T focuses on extending the previous result to cover multiple overlapping variables. The challenge is that when the autoregressive order M increases, the number of overlapping variables between consecutive distributions also increases. For M = 1 , each new factor only shares a single variable with the previous one, so overlaps remain 'local' and can be controlled directly. For M > 1 , however, a single variable may overlap with several different factors at once. This complicates the proof of linear independence under finite mixtures, as we no longer have a single overlap as in Eq. (18). To illustrate, for M = 3 , the trajectory family at T = 6 has the following structure:
texts.text: Linear independence (general case). Our strategy to generalise to any lag M < T focuses on extending the previous result to cover multiple overlapping variables. The challenge is that when the autoregressive order M increases, the number of overlapping variables between consecutive distributions also increases. For M = 1 , each new factor only shares a single variable with the previous one, so overlaps remain 'local' and can be controlled directly. For M > 1 , however, a single variable may overlap with several different factors at once. This complicates the proof of linear independence under finite mixtures, as we no longer have a single overlap as in Eq. (18). To illustrate, for M = 3 , the trajectory family at T = 6 has the following structure:

texts.orig: for every a ∈ A and b 4 , b 5 , b 6 ∈ B , where we indicate overlapping variables with different colours. Compared to M = 1 in Eq. (18), when multiplying the initial and transition distribution, p a ( z 1 , z 2 , z 3 ) p b 4 ( z 4 | z 1 , z 2 , z 3 ) , the overlap ( z 1 , z 2 , z 3 ) has the same structure as in the M = 1 case, and linear independence for T = 4 can be established by LIPO-1. However at T = 5 , the next factor p b 5 ( z 5 | z 4 , z 3 , z 2 ) introduces an overlap ( z 4 , z 3 , z 2 ) that is no longer confined in a single pair of consecutive terms, as ( z 3 , z 2 ) are present in p a ( z 1 , z 2 , z 3 ) . This branching overlap violates assumption (a3) of LIPO-1, as the product family does not directly reduce to the single-overlap structure in Eq. (18). Similar arguments apply for T = 6 , where z 3 overlaps with all the functions.

(18), when multiplying the initial and transition distribution, p a ( z 1 , z 2 , z 3 ) p b 4 ( z 4 | z 1 , z 2 , z 3 ) , the overlap ( z 1 , z 2 , z 3 ) has the same structure as in the M = 1 case, and linear independence for T = 4 can be established by LIPO-1. However at T = 5 , the next factor p b 5 ( z 5 | z 4 , z 3 , z 2 ) introduces an overlap ( z 4 , z 3 , z 2 ) that is no longer confined in a single pair of consecutive terms, as ( z 3 , z 2 ) are present in p a ( z 1 , z 2 , z 3 ) . This branching overlap violates assumption (a3) of LIPO-1, as the product family does not directly reduce to the single-overlap structure in Eq. (18). Similar arguments apply for T = 6 , where z 3 overlaps with all the functions.

texts.orig: As in the case M = 1 linear independence under finite mixtures holds provided that the product ( ⊗ 3 P 3 B ) satisfies assumptions (a4-a6) in LIPO-1. Therefore, the strategy is to impose these assumptions on P M B , and explore whether they extend to the product families ( ⊗ r P M B ) , for all 1 < r ≤ M . In Appendix A.2.2 we prove that such an extension requires strengthening the nonparametric assumptions from (a4-a6) to (b4-b6) (Lemmas 19 and 20). Notably, assumption (b4) requires linear independence under finite mixtures of the family P M B to hold Outer induction: increase trajectory length (Theorem 22)

Therefore, the strategy is to impose these assumptions on P M B , and explore whether they extend to the product families ( ⊗ r P M B ) , for all 1 < r ≤ M . In Appendix A.2.2 we prove that such an extension requires strengthening the nonparametric assumptions from (a4-a6) to (b4-b6) (Lemmas 19 and 20). Notably, assumption (b4) requires linear independence under finite mixtures of the family P M B to hold Outer induction: increase trajectory length (Theorem 22)

texts.text: Linear independence via nested induction. To prove LIPO-M and extend the assumptions to product families (Lemmas 19 and 20), we use induction, with the base case being a single product of consecutive variables ( P M B ⊗P M B ). Therefore, the full proof of linear independence under finite mixtures of the trajectory family requires nested induction (see Theorem 22 in Appendix A.2.4). Figure 4 illustrates this idea:

texts.text: A key technical point in Theorem 22 is that the induction hypothesis applies only when the trajectory length t is a multiple of M . Thus we distinguish two cases:

Assume the prior dynamic model p ( z 1: T ) belongs to the nonlinear Gaussian MSM family M T,M NL specified by Thm. 8. At each time step t , the latent z t ∈ R m generates observed data x t ∈ R n via a piecewise linear transformation f . The generation process of such SDS model can be expressed as follows:
texts.text: Based on identifiable MSMs, our analysis of switching dynamical systems extends the setup from Kivva et al. (2022) (Section 3.2) to the temporal case. Assume the prior dynamic model p ( z 1: T ) belongs to the nonlinear Gaussian MSM family M T,M NL specified by Thm. 8. At each time step t , the latent z t ∈ R m generates observed data x t ∈ R n via a piecewise linear transformation f . The generation process of such SDS model can be expressed as follows:

texts.orig: Definition 10 We say that a function F : R mT → R nT is factored if it is composed of f : R m → R n , such that for any z 1: T ∈ R mT , F ( z 1: T ) = ( f ( z 1 ) , . . . , f ( z T )) ⊤ .
texts.text: Definition 10 We say that a function F : R mT → R nT is factored if it is composed of f : R m → R n , such that for any z 1: T ∈ R mT , F ( z 1: T ) = ( f ( z 1 ) , . . . , f ( z T )) ⊤ .

texts.orig: The identifiability analysis of the SDS model (Eq. (21)) follows two steps (see Figure 5). (i) Following Kivva et al. (2022), we establish the identifiability for a prior p ( z 1: T ) ∈ M T,M NL from ( F # p )( x 1: T ) in the noiseless case. Here F # p denotes the pushforward measure of p by F . (ii) When the noise ε 1: T distribution is known, F # p is identifiable from (( F # p ) ∗ p ε 1: T ) ( x 1: T ) using convolution tricks from Khemakhem et al. (2020) 3 . In detail, we first define the notion of identifiability for p ∈ M T (Π M A , P M B ) given noiseless observations.
texts.text: The identifiability analysis of the SDS model (Eq. (21)) follows two steps (see Figure 5). (i) Following Kivva et al. (2022), we establish the identifiability for a prior p ( z 1: T ) ∈ M T,M NL from ( F # p )( x 1: T ) in the noiseless case. Here F # p denotes the pushforward measure of p by F . (ii) When the noise ε 1: T distribution is known, F # p is identifiable from (( F # p ) ∗ p ε 1: T ) ( x 1: T ) using convolution tricks from Khemakhem et al. (2020) 3 . In detail, we first define the notion of identifiability for p ∈ M T (Π M A , P M B ) given noiseless observations.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 11 Given a family of factored transformations F , for F ∈ F the prior p ∈ M T (Π M A , P M B ) is said to be identifiable up to affine transformations, when for any ˜ F ∈ F and ˜ p ∈ M T (Π M A , P M B ) s.t. F # p = ˜ F # ˜ p , there exists an invertible factored affine mapping H : R mT → R mT composed of h : R m → R m , where p = H # ˜ p . Equivalently, identifiability up to permutation, scaling and translation refers to having h ( x ) = PD x + b , x ∈ R m , where P and D are permutation and diagonal matrices respectively.
texts.text: Definition 11 Given a family of factored transformations F , for F ∈ F the prior p ∈ M T (Π M A , P M B ) is said to be identifiable up to affine transformations, when for any ˜ F ∈ F and ˜ p ∈ M T (Π M A , P M B ) s.t. F # p = ˜ F # ˜ p , there exists an invertible factored affine mapping H : R mT → R mT composed of h : R m → R m , where p = H # ˜ p . Equivalently, identifiability up to permutation, scaling and translation refers to having h ( x ) = PD x + b , x ∈ R m , where P and D are permutation and diagonal matrices respectively.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Relaxing piecewise linearity. While our approach relaxes the injectivity assumption on the emission function, we still restrict f to be piecewise linear. Other works, such as Yao et al. (2022a) assume injective functions, where their results could be adapted to our framework. Specifically, Yao et al. (2022a) establish identifiability under the following conditions: (1) independence of latent variables (s1 in Theorem 12); (2) the existence of a demixing function; and (3) access to K = 2 m +1 components satisfying certain linear independence conditions on vectors formed with derivatives of log p ( z t | z t -1 , . . . , z t -M , k ) , k ∈ [ K ] . However, this strategy either assumes access to the true component label k , or requires imposing assumptions directly on the observed conditional distributions p ( x t | x t -1 , . . . , x t -M , k ) , k ∈ [ K ] . For instance, Song et al. (2023) introduces assumptions (m1-m2) directly on the observed distribution, which implicitly constrain both the latent dynamics and the emission function. This lack of separation between latent structure and observation model makes these assumptions difficult to verify or control in practice (e.g., if observed and latent distributions are Gaussian, then f must be affine). Moreover, without additional structural constraints, these strategies cannot fully exploit our identifiability results for MSMs, which are derived at the level of the latent dynamics. To illustrate, consider the joint probability distribution, an equivalent SDS distribution family would be defined as follows:
texts.text: Relaxing piecewise linearity. While our approach relaxes the injectivity assumption on the emission function, we still restrict f to be piecewise linear. Other works, such as Yao et al. (2022a) assume injective functions, where their results could be adapted to our framework. Specifically, Yao et al. (2022a) establish identifiability under the following conditions: (1) independence of latent variables (s1 in Theorem 12); (2) the existence of a demixing function; and (3) access to K = 2 m +1 components satisfying certain linear independence conditions on vectors formed with derivatives of log p ( z t | z t -1 , . . . , z t -M , k ) , k ∈ [ K ] . However, this strategy either assumes access to the true component label k , or requires imposing assumptions directly on the observed conditional distributions p ( x t | x t -1 , . . . , x t -M , k ) , k ∈ [ K ] . For instance, Song et al. (2023) introduces assumptions (m1-m2) directly on the observed distribution, which implicitly constrain both the latent dynamics and the emission function. This lack of separation between latent structure and observation model makes these assumptions difficult to verify or control in practice (e.g., if observed and latent distributions are Gaussian, then f must be affine). Moreover, without additional structural constraints, these strategies cannot fully exploit our identifiability results for MSMs, which are derived at the level of the latent dynamics. To illustrate, consider the joint probability distribution, an equivalent SDS distribution family would be defined as follows:

texts.orig: 4.3 Regime-dependent Causal Representation Learning
texts.text: 4.3 Regime-dependent Causal Representation Learning

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In regime-dependent time series (Saggioro et al., 2020), the SEM is time-dependent. However, it is causally stationary (Assaad et al., 2022) when conditioned on discrete latent variables s t ∈ [ K ] at each time step t . In the context of LVMs, the SEM operates on the latent space; and therefore, our framework is connected to causal representation learning (Schölkopf et al., 2021). When considering SDSs, the corresponding latent causal structure becomes a regime-dependent causal graph which we denote as G 1: K := { G k } K k =1 ∈ G K . It can be defined as a collection of causal graphs such that for each k ∈ [ K ] , G k ∈ G encodes the latent SEM at time t if s t = k . To establish connections with the previous MSMs, we assume an additive noise model (ANM; (Hoyer et al., 2008)) with absence of instantaneous effects. Therefore, the corresponding regime-dependent SEM in latent space for variable j ∈ [ m ] is expressed as follows:
texts.text: In regime-dependent time series (Saggioro et al., 2020), the SEM is time-dependent. However, it is causally stationary (Assaad et al., 2022) when conditioned on discrete latent variables s t ∈ [ K ] at each time step t . In the context of LVMs, the SEM operates on the latent space; and therefore, our framework is connected to causal representation learning (Schölkopf et al., 2021). When considering SDSs, the corresponding latent causal structure becomes a regime-dependent causal graph which we denote as G 1: K := { G k } K k =1 ∈ G K . It can be defined as a collection of causal graphs such that for each k ∈ [ K ] , G k ∈ G encodes the latent SEM at time t if s t = k . To establish connections with the previous MSMs, we assume an additive noise model (ANM; (Hoyer et al., 2008)) with absence of instantaneous effects. Therefore, the corresponding regime-dependent SEM in latent space for variable j ∈ [ m ] is expressed as follows:

texts.text: (m3) Minimality: if z ( i ) / ∈ Pa ( j ) k ( τ ) , 1 ≤ τ ≤ M , then the i -th dimension of m ( z t -1 , . . . , z t -M , k ) is constant with respect to z ( j ) t -τ .

texts.text: Then, the regime-dependent graph is identifiable up to permutations (Definition 13).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: See Appendix C for the proof. It suffices to show that the regime dependent structure is recovered up to permutations, given the identifiable transition derivatives from Theorems 8 and 12. Under assumptions (m1-m3), we note that other structures in traditional causal time series analysis, such as the full time graph (Peters et al., 2017), are not identifiable in the context of high-order MSMs; as we have no access to the discrete latents s M : T , but rather their distribution up to permutations.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5 Estimation
texts.text: 5 Estimation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We refer to our full approach, which combines the theoretical framework with the inference scheme below, as identifiable Switching Dynamical System (iSDS). For real-world applications (Section 7), we also consider MSMs. Together with our assumptions, we denote the corresponding method as the identifiable Markov Switching Model (iMSM).
texts.text: We refer to our full approach, which combines the theoretical framework with the inference scheme below, as identifiable Switching Dynamical System (iSDS). For real-world applications (Section 7), we also consider MSMs. Together with our assumptions, we denote the corresponding method as the identifiable Markov Switching Model (iMSM).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We adopt the collapsed variational inference setup of Dong et al. (2020). Parameters are learned by maximising the evidence lower bound (ELBO) (Kingma and Welling, 2014). The approximate posterior over latent variables { z 1: T , s M : T } uses a factorisation which incorporates the exact posterior of the discrete latent states conditioned on the continuous latent variables:
texts.text: We adopt the collapsed variational inference setup of Dong et al. (2020). Parameters are learned by maximising the evidence lower bound (ELBO) (Kingma and Welling, 2014). The approximate posterior over latent variables { z 1: T , s M : T } uses a factorisation which incorporates the exact posterior of the discrete latent states conditioned on the continuous latent variables:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: q ϕ , θ ( z 1: T , s M : T | x 1: T ) = T ∏ t =1 q ϕ ( z t | x t ) p θ ( s M : T | z 1: T ) . (24)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The variational posterior over z 1: T factorises across time. This mirrors our injectivity assumptions on the latent variable transformation, and simplifies the estimation. Prior work (Ansari et al., 2021; Dong et al., 2020) constructs q ϕ , θ ( z 1: T | x 1: T ) via a bidirectional RNN on x 1: T , followed by a forward RNN. With the exact posterior p θ ( s M : T | z 1: T ) , we can marginalise s M : T from the ELBO (see Appendix D for details):
texts.text: The variational posterior over z 1: T factorises across time. This mirrors our injectivity assumptions on the latent variable transformation, and simplifies the estimation. Prior work (Ansari et al., 2021; Dong et al., 2020) constructs q ϕ , θ ( z 1: T | x 1: T ) via a bidirectional RNN on x 1: T , followed by a forward RNN. With the exact posterior p θ ( s M : T | z 1: T ) , we can marginalise s M : T from the ELBO (see Appendix D for details):

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: p θ ( x 1: T ) ≥ L ELBO := E q ϕ ( z 1: T | x 1: T ) [ log p θ ( x 1: T | z 1: T ) ] -KL ( q ϕ ( z 1: T | x 1: T ) || p θ ( z 1: T ) ) . (25)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We estimate expectations with Monte Carlo samples ˜ z 1: T ∼ q ϕ ( z 1: T | x 1: T ) and apply the reparametrisation trick for backpropagation (Kingma and Welling, 2014), enabling stochastic gradient ascent. The gradient results as follows:
texts.text: We estimate expectations with Monte Carlo samples ˜ z 1: T ∼ q ϕ ( z 1: T | x 1: T ) and apply the reparametrisation trick for backpropagation (Kingma and Welling, 2014), enabling stochastic gradient ascent. The gradient results as follows:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: ∇L ELBO ≈ ∇ log p θ ( x 1: T | ˜ z 1: T ) -∇ log q ϕ (˜ z 1: T | x 1: T ) + ∇ log p θ (˜ z 1: T ) . (26)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Following Dong et al. (2020), the prior gradient admits an efficient decomposition, similar to standard HMM inference (Bishop, 2006):
texts.text: Following Dong et al. (2020), the prior gradient admits an efficient decomposition, similar to standard HMM inference (Bishop, 2006):

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: ∇ log p θ (˜ z 1: T ) = E p θ ( s M : T | ˜ z 1: T ) [ ∇ log p θ (˜ z 1: T , s M : T ) ] (27)

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: = T ∑ t = M +1 K ∑ k =1 γ t,k (˜ z 1: T ) ∇ log p θ (˜ z t | ˜ z t -1 , . . . , ˜ z t -M , s t = k ) (28)

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: + K ∑ k =1 γ M,k (˜ z 1: T ) ∇ log π k + T ∑ t = M +2 K ∑ k =1 K ∑ k ′ =1 ξ t,k,k ′ (˜ z 1: T ) ∇ log Q k ′ ,k , (29)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where γ t,k (˜ z 1: T ) = p θ ( s t = k | ˜ z 1: T ) and ξ t,k,k ′ (˜ z 1: T ) = p θ ( s t = k, s t -1 = k ′ | ˜ z 1: T ) are computed with forward-backward update rules under a first-order Markov chain over s M : T . See Appendix D for details. A recent alternative (Ansari et al., 2021) computes p ( z 1: T ) using a forward posterior only, but this requires backpropagation through the entire sequence, considerably increasing computational cost.
texts.text: where γ t,k (˜ z 1: T ) = p θ ( s t = k | ˜ z 1: T ) and ξ t,k,k ′ (˜ z 1: T ) = p θ ( s t = k, s t -1 = k ′ | ˜ z 1: T ) are computed with forward-backward update rules under a first-order Markov chain over s M : T . See Appendix D for details. A recent alternative (Ansari et al., 2021) computes p ( z 1: T ) using a forward posterior only, but this requires backpropagation through the entire sequence, considerably increasing computational cost.

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: Identifiability of Regime-Switching Models
texts.text: Identifiability of Regime-Switching Models

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Initialisation and iMSM. Dong et al. (2020) claim that the above approach is prone to state collapse and introduce additional losses with annealing schedules; Ansari et al. (2021) mitigate state collapse via temperature annealing on discrete logits together with a forward posterior. Such schedules are data-dependent and require careful hyperparameter tuning. In our experience, a strong initialisation of p θ ( z 1: T ) is the key to stabilise training and mitigate state collapse. We first project x 1: T into a lower-dimensional space via linear Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and fit a standard MSM to the projected time series following Eq. (29). The learned π, Q , and regime-specific parameters are used to initialise p θ ( z 1: T ) Similar initialisation strategies have proven effective in prior work (Linderman et al., 2016), which serve as a foundation for improved training stability in discrete latent variable models. The iMSM follows the same likelihood-based optimisation, using the gradient decomposition in Eq. (29).
texts.text: Initialisation and iMSM. Dong et al. (2020) claim that the above approach is prone to state collapse and introduce additional losses with annealing schedules; Ansari et al. (2021) mitigate state collapse via temperature annealing on discrete logits together with a forward posterior. Such schedules are data-dependent and require careful hyperparameter tuning. In our experience, a strong initialisation of p θ ( z 1: T ) is the key to stabilise training and mitigate state collapse. We first project x 1: T into a lower-dimensional space via linear Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and fit a standard MSM to the projected time series following Eq. (29). The learned π, Q , and regime-specific parameters are used to initialise p θ ( z 1: T ) Similar initialisation strategies have proven effective in prior work (Linderman et al., 2016), which serve as a foundation for improved training stability in discrete latent variable models. The iMSM follows the same likelihood-based optimisation, using the gradient decomposition in Eq. (29).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Sparsity regularisation. To recover the underlying causal structure, we promote sparsity in the regime-specific mean transitions m ( · , k ) via an element-wise ℓ 1 penalty on their Jacobians J m k for k ∈ [ K ] . The regularised objective is
texts.text: Sparsity regularisation. To recover the underlying causal structure, we promote sparsity in the regime-specific mean transitions m ( · , k ) via an element-wise ℓ 1 penalty on their Jacobians J m k for k ∈ [ K ] . The regularised objective is

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: L REG := L ELBO + η K ∑ k =1 m ∑ i,j ∣ ∣ ∣ ( J m k ) ij ∣ ∣ ∣ , (30)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where m denotes the latent dimension. While maximising the ELBO is a practical estimation approach, it does not guarantee statistical consistency in general; see e.g., Khemakhem et al. (2020) for discussion. Consistency analysis is left to future work.
texts.text: where m denotes the latent dimension. While maximising the ELBO is a practical estimation approach, it does not guarantee statistical consistency in general; see e.g., Khemakhem et al. (2020) for discussion. Consistency analysis is left to future work.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6 Synthetic Experiments
texts.text: 6 Synthetic Experiments

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section, we present experiments on synthetic data, where access to the groundtruth structure enables us to empirically verify the theoretical results. Specifically, we (i) compare the ability of iSDS to recover identifiable latent variables against recent baselines; (ii) investigate the sample and sequence length efficiency; and (iii) test whether the assumptions in Theorem 8 are necessary for identifiability in both MSMs and SDSs.
texts.text: In this section, we present experiments on synthetic data, where access to the groundtruth structure enables us to empirically verify the theoretical results. Specifically, we (i) compare the ability of iSDS to recover identifiable latent variables against recent baselines; (ii) investigate the sample and sequence length efficiency; and (iii) test whether the assumptions in Theorem 8 are necessary for identifiability in both MSMs and SDSs.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Data Generation. We generate data following Balsells-Rodas et al. (2024) mostly with additional scenarios for the noise distribution. The regimes follow a first-order Markov chain, with transition matrix Q that assigns 0 . 9 probability to remain in the same regime, and 0 . 1 probability to transition to the next one. Continuous latent variables z 1: M are initialised from a K -component Gaussian Mixture Model with uniform categorical prior. For t > M , they evolve according to a Gaussian transition model with K regimes and regime-specific parameters. For each k ∈ [ K ] , the mean function m ( · , k ) is given by a two-layer MLP with cosine activations, where each layer is a locally linear map (Zheng et al., 2018), constrained by a randomly generated causal graph G k . Covariance matrices Σ ( · , k ) are diagonal, and we consider three cases:
texts.text: Data Generation. We generate data following Balsells-Rodas et al. (2024) mostly with additional scenarios for the noise distribution. The regimes follow a first-order Markov chain, with transition matrix Q that assigns 0 . 9 probability to remain in the same regime, and 0 . 1 probability to transition to the next one. Continuous latent variables z 1: M are initialised from a K -component Gaussian Mixture Model with uniform categorical prior. For t > M , they evolve according to a Gaussian transition model with K regimes and regime-specific parameters. For each k ∈ [ K ] , the mean function m ( · , k ) is given by a two-layer MLP with cosine activations, where each layer is a locally linear map (Zheng et al., 2018), constrained by a randomly generated causal graph G k . Covariance matrices Σ ( · , k ) are diagonal, and we consider three cases:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Constant noise: fixed, Σ ( · , k ) = Σ .
texts.text: Constant noise: fixed, Σ ( · , k ) = Σ .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Heterogeneous noise: regime-dependent, Σ ( · , k ) = Σ ( k ) .
texts.text: Heterogeneous noise: regime-dependent, Σ ( · , k ) = Σ ( k ) .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · History-dependent noise: diagonal entries proportional to the scaled absolute value of the mean.
texts.text: History-dependent noise: diagonal entries proportional to the scaled absolute value of the mean.

Observations are generated by passing latent variables through a two-layer MLP with Leaky ReLU activations. Unless otherwise stated, we generate N = 10000 samples for training, and 1000 samples for evaluation, both with length T = 100 .
texts.text: The latter two satisfy assumption (s2), i.e., distinct diagonal ratios across at least one pair of regimes. Observations are generated by passing latent variables through a two-layer MLP with Leaky ReLU activations. Unless otherwise stated, we generate N = 10000 samples for training, and 1000 samples for evaluation, both with length T = 100 .

texts.orig: Metrics. To evaluate the learned solutions against ground truth, we use the following metrics (details in Appendix E.3):
texts.text: Metrics. To evaluate the learned solutions against ground truth, we use the following metrics (details in Appendix E.3):

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Regime estimation: the component with highest posterior probability is selected, and the F 1 score is computed after regime permutation alignment.
texts.text: Regime estimation: the component with highest posterior probability is selected, and the F 1 score is computed after regime permutation alignment.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Continuous latent variable alignment: we report both weak and strong mean correlation coefficients (MCC). The strong MCC, also defined in Khemakhem et al. (2020), is a standard measure in ICA and evaluates alignment up to scaling and permutation. In contrast, weak MCC (Kivva et al., 2022) allows affine transformations.
texts.text: Continuous latent variable alignment: we report both weak and strong mean correlation coefficients (MCC). The strong MCC, also defined in Khemakhem et al. (2020), is a standard measure in ICA and evaluates alignment up to scaling and permutation. In contrast, weak MCC (Kivva et al., 2022) allows affine transformations.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Causal structure recovery: the F 1 score between ground-truth and estimated causal graphs. This is obtained by thresholding the Jacobian after regime and continuous latent variable permutation alignment.
texts.text: Causal structure recovery: the F 1 score between ground-truth and estimated causal graphs. This is obtained by thresholding the Jacobian after regime and continuous latent variable permutation alignment.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Transition functions: the averaged L 2 distance and R 2 score between ground-truth and estimated means m ( · , k ) after regime permutation and latent variable affine alignment.
texts.text: Transition functions: the averaged L 2 distance and R 2 score between ground-truth and estimated means m ( · , k ) after regime permutation and latent variable affine alignment.

texts.text: SNICA (Hälvä et al., 2021), based on linear switching dynamical systems, where each latent variable follows its own SDS.

texts.orig: Figure 6 shows the weak and strong MCC scores for each configuration (A-F) across 10 dataset seeds. We generally observe that iSDS outperforms the baselines in terms of weak MCC, achieving near-perfect affine alignment in all settings. This is followed by SNICA and NCTRL, as expected since both are SDS-based approaches. Regarding strong MCC, iSDS again outperforms all baselines, although performance drops for settings A, and D-F. In A-C the only difference lies in noise distribution. Lower strong MCC in A is expected, since identifiability up to scaling and permutation are not guaranteed under constant covariance matrices. In D-F, the lower MCC scores can be attributed to increased complexity from higher lags, dimensions, regimes, and nonstationary noise. For regime estimation, Figure 7a compares iSDS with NCTRL and CtrlNS as they extract regimes in a comparable way. iSDS consistently achieves superior F 1 scores, where performance decreases with more regimes (E and F). NCTRL performs significantly worse, despite being based on SDSs. This can be explained because NCTRL estimates regimes directly from observations under Gaussianity assumptions, which is a model mismatch as the generated observations are generally non-Gaussian. Finally, Figure 7b reports causal structure recovery. In A-C, the F 1 score improves with increasing nonstationarity of the noise, consistent with our theoretical results. In contrast, settings D-F show reduced performance in line with the previously reported MCC scores.
texts.text: Figure 6 shows the weak and strong MCC scores for each configuration (A-F) across 10 dataset seeds. We generally observe that iSDS outperforms the baselines in terms of weak MCC, achieving near-perfect affine alignment in all settings. This is followed by SNICA and NCTRL, as expected since both are SDS-based approaches. Regarding strong MCC, iSDS again outperforms all baselines, although performance drops for settings A, and D-F. In A-C the only difference lies in noise distribution. Lower strong MCC in A is expected, since identifiability up to scaling and permutation are not guaranteed under constant covariance matrices. In D-F, the lower MCC scores can be attributed to increased complexity from higher lags, dimensions, regimes, and nonstationary noise. For regime estimation, Figure 7a compares iSDS with NCTRL and CtrlNS as they extract regimes in a comparable way. iSDS consistently achieves superior F 1 scores, where performance decreases with more regimes (E and F). NCTRL performs significantly worse, despite being based on SDSs. This can be explained because NCTRL estimates regimes directly from observations under Gaussianity assumptions, which is a model mismatch as the generated observations are generally non-Gaussian. Finally, Figure 7b reports causal structure recovery. In A-C, the F 1 score improves with increasing nonstationarity of the noise, consistent with our theoretical results. In contrast, settings D-F show reduced performance in line with the previously reported MCC scores.

texts.orig: Data efficiency analysis. To better understand the sample and sequence efficiency of iSDS, we generate data with n = m = 5 latent and observed dimensions, M = 2 lags, K = 3 regimes, and constant noise. We evaluate the F 1 score of the estimated regimes, and the average L 2 distance of mean functions m ( · ) after affine alignment. Results are shown in Figures 8a and 8b, where we vary dataset size and sequence length, respectively. In Figure 8a, the sequence length is fixed to T = 100 , and in Figure 8b, the number of samples is fixed to N = 10000 .
texts.text: Data efficiency analysis. To better understand the sample and sequence efficiency of iSDS, we generate data with n = m = 5 latent and observed dimensions, M = 2 lags, K = 3 regimes, and constant noise. We evaluate the F 1 score of the estimated regimes, and the average L 2 distance of mean functions m ( · ) after affine alignment. Results are shown in Figures 8a and 8b, where we vary dataset size and sequence length, respectively. In Figure 8a, the sequence length is fixed to T = 100 , and in Figure 8b, the number of samples is fixed to N = 10000 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We observe that larger sample sizes substantially improve performance in terms of both regime recovery and mean function estimation. However, increasing the sequence length has little effect for a sufficient number of samples. The results suggest that, when training iSDS, collecting more sequences is generally more beneficial than increasing their length.
texts.text: We observe that larger sample sizes substantially improve performance in terms of both regime recovery and mean function estimation. However, increasing the sequence length has little effect for a sufficient number of samples. The results suggest that, when training iSDS, collecting more sequences is generally more beneficial than increasing their length.

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: Identifiability of Regime-Switching Models
texts.text: Identifiability of Regime-Switching Models

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Selecting K and M . Our theoretical framework establishes that for both iMSM and iSDS, the number of regimes K is identifiable. Assuming consistency holds for iSDS, our results facilitate learning the ground truth K by model selection (McLachlan and Peel, 2000). Similar arguments apply for selecting M , where for a smaller lag the model becomes misspecified, and for larger M , extra lags may be unused. We show this experimentally, and leave more thorough theoretical analyses for future work. To select K and M we use the elbow method (Thorndike, 1953), which is a standard heuristic for finite mixture models. For iSDS, we use the ELBO on a held-out dataset, rather than the log-likelihood which could be possible for iMSM. Figure 9 showcases a model selection example for iSDS with M = 2 lags and K = 3 where we increase the estimated regimes ˆ K while maintaining the ground-truth lag M (yellow line), increase the estimated lag ˆ M while maintaining the ground-truth number of regimes (blue line). In both cases, we observe that the highest test ELBO is reached at the ground-truth M and K , and the performance plateaus for higher choices of ˆ M and ˆ K .
texts.text: Selecting K and M . Our theoretical framework establishes that for both iMSM and iSDS, the number of regimes K is identifiable. Assuming consistency holds for iSDS, our results facilitate learning the ground truth K by model selection (McLachlan and Peel, 2000). Similar arguments apply for selecting M , where for a smaller lag the model becomes misspecified, and for larger M , extra lags may be unused. We show this experimentally, and leave more thorough theoretical analyses for future work. To select K and M we use the elbow method (Thorndike, 1953), which is a standard heuristic for finite mixture models. For iSDS, we use the ELBO on a held-out dataset, rather than the log-likelihood which could be possible for iMSM. Figure 9 showcases a model selection example for iSDS with M = 2 lags and K = 3 where we increase the estimated regimes ˆ K while maintaining the ground-truth lag M (yellow line), increase the estimated lag ˆ M while maintaining the ground-truth number of regimes (blue line). In both cases, we observe that the highest test ELBO is reached at the ground-truth M and K , and the performance plateaus for higher choices of ˆ M and ˆ K .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Ablations. Section 4.1 establishes identifiability for parametric MSMs with analytic functions and unique indexing. For M = 1 , a sufficient condition is the existence of an open set where regime-specific mean (and covariance) functions do not intersect. However, for general M , we require this set to have full measure. These are sufficient, but not proven necessary, conditions. We assess necessity experimentally by designing two settings for both iMSM and iSDS: (i) Zero , where the ground-truth mean functions intersect at most on a zero measure set; and (ii) Overlap , where functions coincide on a set with nonzero measure but differ elsewhere. We consider n = m = 5 , K = 3 , constant noise, and lags M ∈ { 1 , 3 , 5 } .
texts.text: Ablations. Section 4.1 establishes identifiability for parametric MSMs with analytic functions and unique indexing. For M = 1 , a sufficient condition is the existence of an open set where regime-specific mean (and covariance) functions do not intersect. However, for general M , we require this set to have full measure. These are sufficient, but not proven necessary, conditions. We assess necessity experimentally by designing two settings for both iMSM and iSDS: (i) Zero , where the ground-truth mean functions intersect at most on a zero measure set; and (ii) Overlap , where functions coincide on a set with nonzero measure but differ elsewhere. We consider n = m = 5 , K = 3 , constant noise, and lags M ∈ { 1 , 3 , 5 } .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure 10 reports the average R 2 of the mean functions m ( · ) after regime permutation alignment (with an additional affine alignment for iSDS). Using R 2 rather than L 2 facilitates comparisons across lags with different input/output scales. As expected, Zero outperforms Overlap . However, Overlap shows strong fits with high R 2 scores, far from indicating model failure. For iSDS, the affine alignment can cause the R 2 scores to be higher. However, for iMSM we observe strong fits for M = 5 . This suggests that the stronger full measure non-intersection is sufficient but not necessary in practice, as otherwise we would obtain much lower R 2 scores (e.g., R 2 < 0 . 5 ). Therefore, the decrease in performance in Overlap can be attributed to estimation challenges from the overlap region, where tracking the regimes is more difficult, rather than to a fundamental lack of identifiability.
texts.text: Figure 10 reports the average R 2 of the mean functions m ( · ) after regime permutation alignment (with an additional affine alignment for iSDS). Using R 2 rather than L 2 facilitates comparisons across lags with different input/output scales. As expected, Zero outperforms Overlap . However, Overlap shows strong fits with high R 2 scores, far from indicating model failure. For iSDS, the affine alignment can cause the R 2 scores to be higher. However, for iMSM we observe strong fits for M = 5 . This suggests that the stronger full measure non-intersection is sufficient but not necessary in practice, as otherwise we would obtain much lower R 2 scores (e.g., R 2 < 0 . 5 ). Therefore, the decrease in performance in Overlap can be attributed to estimation challenges from the overlap region, where tracking the regimes is more difficult, rather than to a fundamental lack of identifiability.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 7 Applications to Real-World Data
texts.text: 7 Applications to Real-World Data

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Having established the validity of our theoretical results under controlled conditions, we now turn to real-world data, where ground-truth structure is generally unavailable. In this section, we therefore focus on assessing the applicability of the theory developed in Section 4 in a fully unsupervised setting. We study datasets from diverse scientific domains and investigate whether assumptions on smoothness and nonstationarity (i.e., assumptions (m2) and (s2)) facilitate learning structured latent representations. Importantly, our identifiability-based framework establishes that learned representations are one-to-one with respect to observations, up to acceptable equivalences (e.g., scaling, permutation). This uniqueness property allows a more trustworthy interpretation of the recovered structures, allowing us to relate them to established domain knowledge. Overall, our aim in this section is to demonstrate how the proposed iMSM and iSDS can support scientific discovery in real-world data.
texts.text: Having established the validity of our theoretical results under controlled conditions, we now turn to real-world data, where ground-truth structure is generally unavailable. In this section, we therefore focus on assessing the applicability of the theory developed in Section 4 in a fully unsupervised setting. We study datasets from diverse scientific domains and investigate whether assumptions on smoothness and nonstationarity (i.e., assumptions (m2) and (s2)) facilitate learning structured latent representations. Importantly, our identifiability-based framework establishes that learned representations are one-to-one with respect to observations, up to acceptable equivalences (e.g., scaling, permutation). This uniqueness property allows a more trustworthy interpretation of the recovered structures, allowing us to relate them to established domain knowledge. Overall, our aim in this section is to demonstrate how the proposed iMSM and iSDS can support scientific discovery in real-world data.
