texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 7.1 Brain Activity Data
texts.text: 7.1 Brain Activity Data

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We first illustrate iMSM on electrocorticography (ECoG) recordings from the NeuroTycho database 4 , originally presented by Yanagawa et al. (2013). Signals from 128 electrodes were recorded in a macaque monkey under two conditions: normal wakefulness ( Awake ) and propofol-induced loss of consciousness ( Anaesthetised ). Following prior work (Mediano et al., 2023), we expect the awake condition to exhibit more complex dynamics, reflected in more frequent transitions between latent regimes.
texts.text: We first illustrate iMSM on electrocorticography (ECoG) recordings from the NeuroTycho database 4 , originally presented by Yanagawa et al. (2013). Signals from 128 electrodes were recorded in a macaque monkey under two conditions: normal wakefulness ( Awake ) and propofol-induced loss of consciousness ( Anaesthetised ). Following prior work (Mediano et al., 2023), we expect the awake condition to exhibit more complex dynamics, reflected in more frequent transitions between latent regimes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The raw ECoG is sampled at 1kHz and comprises Awake and Anaesthetised segments, each lasting 929.8 and 650.65 seconds respectively. We select 21 electrodes approximately covering the visual cortex of the brain. We first apply a second-order Butterworth notch filter at 50Hz to eliminate line noise. Then, we downsample the data to 200Hz, standardise each channel independently, and chunk each sequence into epochs of 2 seconds ( T = 400 ). This yields 464 Awake and 325 Anaesthetised segments, with 50 from each condition held out for testing.
texts.text: The raw ECoG is sampled at 1kHz and comprises Awake and Anaesthetised segments, each lasting 929.8 and 650.65 seconds respectively. We select 21 electrodes approximately covering the visual cortex of the brain. We first apply a second-order Butterworth notch filter at 50Hz to eliminate line noise. Then, we downsample the data to 200Hz, standardise each channel independently, and chunk each sequence into epochs of 2 seconds ( T = 400 ). This yields 464 Awake and 325 Anaesthetised segments, with 50 from each condition held out for testing.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In both data settings, we set K = 15 and parametrise transition functions with cosine activations (model selection details in Appendix E.4). Figure 11a reports test log-likelihoods for iMSM as a function of lag order M . As expected, Awake is consistently harder to model (lower log-likelihoods), indicating more complex dynamics. iMSM with M = 2 achieves the best performance in both settings. Notably, the Anaesthetised condition remains competitive at M = 1 but degrades rapidly for M > 2 , consistent with simpler dynamics. In contrast, Awake benefits significantly from higher lag orders.
texts.text: In both data settings, we set K = 15 and parametrise transition functions with cosine activations (model selection details in Appendix E.4). Figure 11a reports test log-likelihoods for iMSM as a function of lag order M . As expected, Awake is consistently harder to model (lower log-likelihoods), indicating more complex dynamics. iMSM with M = 2 achieves the best performance in both settings. Notably, the Anaesthetised condition remains competitive at M = 1 but degrades rapidly for M > 2 , consistent with simpler dynamics. In contrast, Awake benefits significantly from higher lag orders.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure 11b illustrates posterior regime occupancy for 2s epochs in each condition (smoothed by a length3 uniform kernel). We only show regimes with more than 1% occupancy in the epoch. Anaesthetised shows prolonged stays within a smaller subset of regimes, whereas Awake occupies a larger fraction of regimes with more frequent transitions. Table 1 quantifies these differences: relative to Anaesthetised , Awake uses more regimes overall and per sample, exhibits higher transition frequency (Hz), and has shorter regime durations on average.
texts.text: Figure 11b illustrates posterior regime occupancy for 2s epochs in each condition (smoothed by a length3 uniform kernel). We only show regimes with more than 1% occupancy in the epoch. Anaesthetised shows prolonged stays within a smaller subset of regimes, whereas Awake occupies a larger fraction of regimes with more frequent transitions. Table 1 quantifies these differences: relative to Anaesthetised , Awake uses more regimes overall and per sample, exhibits higher transition frequency (Hz), and has shorter regime durations on average.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Crucially, iMSM provides regime identifiability guarantees to ensure the differences in regime usage reflect condition-specific structure supported by the data. This highlights the importance of considering iMSM or iSDS for neuroscience time series such as ECoG, complementing existing methodologies (Mediano et al., 2023).
texts.text: Crucially, iMSM provides regime identifiability guarantees to ensure the differences in regime usage reflect condition-specific structure supported by the data. This highlights the importance of considering iMSM or iSDS for neuroscience time series such as ECoG, complementing existing methodologies (Mediano et al., 2023).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 7.2 Financial Data
texts.text: 7.2 Financial Data

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Financial time series are a direct application area for regime-switching models. Early developments in MSMs (Hamilton, 1989) focused on modelling market-cycle regimes and laid the foundations for regime-switching models in finance. In this section, we study US sector-based stock price data and use iSDS to extract structured latent representations and regimes. Related applications are considered with both MSMs (Alizadeh and Nomikos, 2004; Cavaliere et al., 2014; Mahmoudi and Ghaneei, 2022) and SDSs (Azzouzi and Nabney, 1999; Carvalho and Lopes, 2007; Fox et al., 2011). Empirically, researchers typically work with log returns or log price(-dividend) .
texts.text: Financial time series are a direct application area for regime-switching models. Early developments in MSMs (Hamilton, 1989) focused on modelling market-cycle regimes and laid the foundations for regime-switching models in finance. In this section, we study US sector-based stock price data and use iSDS to extract structured latent representations and regimes. Related applications are considered with both MSMs (Alizadeh and Nomikos, 2004; Cavaliere et al., 2014; Mahmoudi and Ghaneei, 2022) and SDSs (Azzouzi and Nabney, 1999; Carvalho and Lopes, 2007; Fox et al., 2011). Empirically, researchers typically work with log returns or log price(-dividend) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Log returns r t = log Ä p t p t -1 ä are often treated as stationary (Cont, 2001), and regimeswitching mainly focuses on regime-dependent means and/or volatilities. This includes MSMs combined with conditional heteroskedasticity (e.g., GARCH (Bollerslev, 1986)), where the conditional variance depends on latent variables and/or past returns (Ang and Bekaert, 2002; Carvalho and Lopes, 2007). Such models often interpret states as bull/bear or tranquil/turbulent and are widely used for risk assessment and allocation (Guidolin and Timmermann, 2007).
texts.text: Log returns r t = log Ä p t p t -1 ä are often treated as stationary (Cont, 2001), and regimeswitching mainly focuses on regime-dependent means and/or volatilities. This includes MSMs combined with conditional heteroskedasticity (e.g., GARCH (Bollerslev, 1986)), where the conditional variance depends on latent variables and/or past returns (Ang and Bekaert, 2002; Carvalho and Lopes, 2007). Such models often interpret states as bull/bear or tranquil/turbulent and are widely used for risk assessment and allocation (Guidolin and Timmermann, 2007).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Because prices are typically nonstationary, regime-switching on log price data is often modelled through present-value frameworks that separate company fundamentals (e.g., earnings) from speculative components (Balke and Wohar, 2009; Chan and Santi, 2021). These models capture longer-horizon regimes (persistent bear/bull phases), and speculative episodes.
texts.text: Because prices are typically nonstationary, regime-switching on log price data is often modelled through present-value frameworks that separate company fundamentals (e.g., earnings) from speculative components (Balke and Wohar, 2009; Chan and Santi, 2021). These models capture longer-horizon regimes (persistent bear/bull phases), and speculative episodes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Data modality and identifiability. We analyse both data modalities (log returns and log prices) to illustrate our theory in a real-world setting. Our SDS results imply that regimes are identifiable under suitable modelling conditions (Theorem 12). For continuous latents, identifiability up to permutation and scaling requires certain heterogeneous or historydependent noise (Theorem 12; Corollary 14). More specifically, it requires for a pair of regimes to have distinct noise ratios across dimensions. In practice, nonstationary log prices are more likely to satisfy the above conditions due to regime and sector-specific scales. In contrast, the noise scales in log returns are normalised, since they are ratios of adjacent prices. This implies that even history-dependent volatility might not meet the required conditions for permutation and scaling identifiability of the latent variables. We therefore evaluate both settings to assess which one captures better structured representations in iSDS.
texts.text: Data modality and identifiability. We analyse both data modalities (log returns and log prices) to illustrate our theory in a real-world setting. Our SDS results imply that regimes are identifiable under suitable modelling conditions (Theorem 12). For continuous latents, identifiability up to permutation and scaling requires certain heterogeneous or historydependent noise (Theorem 12; Corollary 14). More specifically, it requires for a pair of regimes to have distinct noise ratios across dimensions. In practice, nonstationary log prices are more likely to satisfy the above conditions due to regime and sector-specific scales. In contrast, the noise scales in log returns are normalised, since they are ratios of adjacent prices. This implies that even history-dependent volatility might not meet the required conditions for permutation and scaling identifiability of the latent variables. We therefore evaluate both settings to assess which one captures better structured representations in iSDS.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Dataset and model setting. We source 100 U.S. stock prices from Bloomberg Terminal (Bloomberg L.P., 2025), grouped into 10 sectors, and retain daily prices for the past ten years (Jan. 2015-May 2025). This yields a sequence of 2630 time steps. See Appendix E.5 for the company list and sector mapping. For training, we draw random subsequences of T = 200 , from the first 2000 time steps, and we leave the remaining 630 for testing. We train iSDS with 11 latent variables and 3 regimes: ten sector latents (one per sector) plus a global latent, and three market regimes intended to capture, e.g., bull/bear/sideways or high/mid/low volatility, similar to related works on financial time series (Chan and Santi, 2021). We set M = 5 and use heterogeneous covariances, i.e., Σ ( · , k ) = Σ ( k ) for k ∈ [ K ] . See Appendix E.2 for additional training details.
texts.text: Dataset and model setting. We source 100 U.S. stock prices from Bloomberg Terminal (Bloomberg L.P., 2025), grouped into 10 sectors, and retain daily prices for the past ten years (Jan. 2015-May 2025). This yields a sequence of 2630 time steps. See Appendix E.5 for the company list and sector mapping. For training, we draw random subsequences of T = 200 , from the first 2000 time steps, and we leave the remaining 630 for testing. We train iSDS with 11 latent variables and 3 regimes: ten sector latents (one per sector) plus a global latent, and three market regimes intended to capture, e.g., bull/bear/sideways or high/mid/low volatility, similar to related works on financial time series (Chan and Santi, 2021). We set M = 5 and use heterogeneous covariances, i.e., Σ ( · , k ) = Σ ( k ) for k ∈ [ K ] . See Appendix E.2 for additional training details.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Results on log returns. Figure 12 shows the training/test segmentation overlaid on the S&P 500 for context. When trained on log returns, iSDS exhibits regimes capturing short-horizon volatility adaptation, where green, yellow, and red colours correspond to low, medium, and high volatility, respectively. We observe a prolonged low-volatility period from late 2016 to early 2018 and a significantly long high-volatility regime in March 2020 (COVID19). From 2020 onward, the segmentation consists mainly of medium and high-volatility regimes, consistent with the increased variability of the S&P 500.

texts.orig: To assess latent structure, Figure 13 displays the decoder Jacobian (latents → assets), grouped by sector. The pattern shows little sector-specific structure: multiple latent variables mapping to the same sector (e.g., significant concentration around the 'Materials" sector), while some latents (e.g., z 3 and z 7 ) are weakly used. This aligns with our theory: the ratio in log returns normalises noise scales across dimensions, preventing disentanglement theoretically.
texts.text: To assess latent structure, Figure 13 displays the decoder Jacobian (latents → assets), grouped by sector. The pattern shows little sector-specific structure: multiple latent variables mapping to the same sector (e.g., significant concentration around the 'Materials" sector), while some latents (e.g., z 3 and z 7 ) are weakly used. This aligns with our theory: the ratio in log returns normalises noise scales across dimensions, preventing disentanglement theoretically.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Results on log prices. Figure 14 reports the corresponding segmentation for log prices. Here, iSDS extracts longer-horizon regimes: a green state aligned with bull periods, and a yellow state aligned with lateral/bear phases. Compared to the returns model, the inferred regimes are more persistent and track macroeconomic phases, consistent with the literature on present-value frameworks (Chan and Santi, 2021).
texts.text: Results on log prices. Figure 14 reports the corresponding segmentation for log prices. Here, iSDS extracts longer-horizon regimes: a green state aligned with bull periods, and a yellow state aligned with lateral/bear phases. Compared to the returns model, the inferred regimes are more persistent and track macroeconomic phases, consistent with the literature on present-value frameworks (Chan and Santi, 2021).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The decoder Jacobian (Fig. 15) shows a significantly clearer structure than in the returns case (cf. Fig. 13). Several latents align with sectors or sector pairs (e.g., z 5 : Materials/Consumer Staples; z 5 , z 7 : Consumer Staples; z 8 : Real Estate; z 4 , z 6 : Health Care). Other latents ( z 2 and z 1 0 ) behave as global factors. Some sectors (e.g., Technology and Industrials) spread across multiple latents, which is plausible given their cyclicality and sensitivity to market conditions. In general, the log price model yields a more structured, sector-aware representation, which is aligned with our identifiability expectations. The lack of stronger sector-specific disentanglement is also consistent with our synthetic results, where iSDS did not achieve high strong MCC scores.
texts.text: The decoder Jacobian (Fig. 15) shows a significantly clearer structure than in the returns case (cf. Fig. 13). Several latents align with sectors or sector pairs (e.g., z 5 : Materials/Consumer Staples; z 5 , z 7 : Consumer Staples; z 8 : Real Estate; z 4 , z 6 : Health Care). Other latents ( z 2 and z 1 0 ) behave as global factors. Some sectors (e.g., Technology and Industrials) spread across multiple latents, which is plausible given their cyclicality and sensitivity to market conditions. In general, the log price model yields a more structured, sector-aware representation, which is aligned with our identifiability expectations. The lack of stronger sector-specific disentanglement is also consistent with our synthetic results, where iSDS did not achieve high strong MCC scores.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Summary. Comparing both modalities, iSDS on log returns discovers short-time volatility regimes with weak sector disentanglement, while log prices yield more persistent macro regimes with clearer sector-aligned latents. The stronger structured representation in the log price modality is consistent with the nonstationarity of the data and our heterogeneity requirements for identifiable representations. For additional details, in Appendix E.5 we also investigate the learned latent structures of iSDS in both data modalities.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 7.3 Climate Data
texts.text: 7.3 Climate Data

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Climate data is characterised by strong seasonal patterns and regime-dependent approaches have recently been explored in this context (Saggioro et al., 2020). Modelling Earth systems is crucial to understand climatological dynamics and to detect extreme events. In this section, we investigate high-dimensional spatio-temporal climate data to demonstrate the applicability of our theoretical framework to this scientific domain.
texts.text: Climate data is characterised by strong seasonal patterns and regime-dependent approaches have recently been explored in this context (Saggioro et al., 2020). Modelling Earth systems is crucial to understand climatological dynamics and to detect extreme events. In this section, we investigate high-dimensional spatio-temporal climate data to demonstrate the applicability of our theoretical framework to this scientific domain.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We consider the Normalised Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) over Africa, following a setup similar to that of Varando et al. (2022). The NDVI measures vegetation greenness as the normalised ratio between near-infrared and red reflectance, and is widely used as a proxy for vegetation productivity and land-surface phenology (Pettorelli, 2013). We use the MODIS/Terra Vegetation Indices 16-Day L3 Global 0.05Deg CMG V006 product (Didan, 2015) together with the ENSO 3.4 climate index, both provided in preprocessed form by Varando et al. (2022). The dataset follows similar preprocessing described in their work: NDVI is reprojected to a 25km Equal-Area Scalable Earth (EASE) grid 5 , retaining the pixels corresponding to Africa (bounding box [ -20 , 55] , [ -37 , 38] in [latitude, longitude]). For each year, the product provides NDVI values from day 1 to 353 every 16 days. We select the period 2001-2020. Missing values are filled temporally (per pixel) using linear interpolation. The resulting dataset is a video tensor with 460 time steps and 289 × 356 pixels. The ENSO 3.4 index is sampled to match the 16-day temporal resolution. Since the index is available daily, a 16-day sliding window is used, followed by 16 × downsampling. With these datasets, we expect our iMSM and iSDS to extract regimes corresponding to seasonal dynamics and, similarly to Granger-PCA (Varando et al., 2022), investigate whether the learned latent variables align with ENSO 3.4.
texts.text: We consider the Normalised Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) over Africa, following a setup similar to that of Varando et al. (2022). The NDVI measures vegetation greenness as the normalised ratio between near-infrared and red reflectance, and is widely used as a proxy for vegetation productivity and land-surface phenology (Pettorelli, 2013). We use the MODIS/Terra Vegetation Indices 16-Day L3 Global 0.05Deg CMG V006 product (Didan, 2015) together with the ENSO 3.4 climate index, both provided in preprocessed form by Varando et al. (2022). The dataset follows similar preprocessing described in their work: NDVI is reprojected to a 25km Equal-Area Scalable Earth (EASE) grid 5 , retaining the pixels corresponding to Africa (bounding box [ -20 , 55] , [ -37 , 38] in [latitude, longitude]). For each year, the product provides NDVI values from day 1 to 353 every 16 days. We select the period 2001-2020. Missing values are filled temporally (per pixel) using linear interpolation. The resulting dataset is a video tensor with 460 time steps and 289 × 356 pixels. The ENSO 3.4 index is sampled to match the 16-day temporal resolution. Since the index is available daily, a 16-day sliding window is used, followed by 16 × downsampling. With these datasets, we expect our iMSM and iSDS to extract regimes corresponding to seasonal dynamics and, similarly to Granger-PCA (Varando et al., 2022), investigate whether the learned latent variables align with ENSO 3.4.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Model setting. We select the 360 most recent time steps, and reserve the first 100 for testing and model selection. We train iMSM following Varando et al. (2022), where we use PCA on the land pixels (a total of 56196 pixels) and retain 10 principal components. For iSDS, we use random 64 × 64 patches from land pixels, and use CNN-MLP encoder-decoder. Here we consider 20 latent variables to account for additional spatial features on the continent shape. Given the inherent seasonality in climate, we assume known K = 4 (one regime per season). Through model selection, we set heterogeneous covariances, GELU activations in the transitions, M = 1 for iMSM, and M = 2 for iSDS. See Appendices E.2 and E.6 for additional training and model selection details, respectively.
texts.text: Model setting. We select the 360 most recent time steps, and reserve the first 100 for testing and model selection. We train iMSM following Varando et al. (2022), where we use PCA on the land pixels (a total of 56196 pixels) and retain 10 principal components. For iSDS, we use random 64 × 64 patches from land pixels, and use CNN-MLP encoder-decoder. Here we consider 20 latent variables to account for additional spatial features on the continent shape. Given the inherent seasonality in climate, we assume known K = 4 (one regime per season). Through model selection, we set heterogeneous covariances, GELU activations in the transitions, M = 1 for iMSM, and M = 2 for iSDS. See Appendices E.2 and E.6 for additional training and model selection details, respectively.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Segmentation results. Figure 16 shows segmentation results of both models. Because iMSM is trained on 10 principal components, the extracted regimes act globally across the continent. We observe three coherent seasonal regimes recurring each year: Mar-May, Jun-Aug, and Sep-Feb. In contrast, iSDS yields region-dependent regime structures: one regime is predominant over the Sahara and Congo (patches 1 and 4 in Figure 16A); while two/three regimes alternate over Ethiopia, the Sahel, and South Africa (patches 2, 3, and 5 in Figure 16B). Although the spatial regime structure differs across regions, the underlying regime effects are shared in iSDS: for example, South Africa's segmentation is approximately the opposite of the Sahel/Ethiopia segmentations. Visualisations of global and regional year-based structures are provided in Appendix E.6.
texts.text: Segmentation results. Figure 16 shows segmentation results of both models. Because iMSM is trained on 10 principal components, the extracted regimes act globally across the continent. We observe three coherent seasonal regimes recurring each year: Mar-May, Jun-Aug, and Sep-Feb. Although the spatial regime structure differs across regions, the underlying regime effects are shared in iSDS: for example, South Africa's segmentation is approximately the opposite of the Sahel/Ethiopia segmentations. Visualisations of global and regional year-based structures are provided in Appendix E.6.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Correlation with ENSO. To quantitatively assess the learned regime-dependent dynamics, we relate the learned latents to the preprocessed ENSO 3.4 time series. For iMSM (global), we split the time series into seasonal slices determined by the regimes and compute lagged Pearson correlations between each PCA component and ENSO 3.4 over lags ranging from 0 -240 days ( 16 -day steps; ENSO leads), and also report the 'All times' baseline (i.e., no splits). For iSDS (regional), we sample 10 64 × 64 land patches per region, compute latent-ENSO correlation matrices (latents × lags), and combine them with Fisher z -averaging. For each season/region, we report the peak correlation strength and its lag. Full correlation matrices, and additional visualisations are provided in Appendix E.6.
texts.text: Correlation with ENSO. To quantitatively assess the learned regime-dependent dynamics, we relate the learned latents to the preprocessed ENSO 3.4 time series. For iMSM (global), we split the time series into seasonal slices determined by the regimes and compute lagged Pearson correlations between each PCA component and ENSO 3.4 over lags ranging from 0 -240 days ( 16 -day steps; ENSO leads), and also report the 'All times' baseline (i.e., no splits). For iSDS (regional), we sample 10 64 × 64 land patches per region, compute latent-ENSO correlation matrices (latents × lags), and combine them with Fisher z -averaging. For each season/region, we report the peak correlation strength and its lag. Full correlation matrices, and additional visualisations are provided in Appendix E.6.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The results are summarised in Tables 2 and 3. For iMSM (c.f. Table 2), we observe regime-dependent splits yield clearer peaks than the 'All times' baseline. This indicates that aggregating across seasons attenuates the lagged effects of ENSO on the time series. Our results show that iMSM segments the time series into regimes where the effects of ENSO can be perceived with different lags. For iSDS (c.f. Table 3), the regional responses are heterogeneous. We observe that Sahara, Congo and Sahel are not significantly aligned with ENSO. On the other hand, South Africa shows slightly higher correlation with ENSO in the South African Summer compared to Winter. Furthermore, Ethiopia shows a very high correlation with ENSO during the months of January-March. In climate analysis, researchers study effects of phenomena such as ENSO across lag scales (Rouault and Tomety, 2022), and within different regions (e.g., Sahel (Nicholson, 2013) and Ethiopia (Bekele-Biratu et al., 2018). In general, iMSM and iSDS can provide noteworthy insights on external drivers such as ENSO, as they decouple both seasonal and regional effects.

In general, iMSM and iSDS can provide noteworthy insights on external drivers such as ENSO, as they decouple both seasonal and regional effects.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Summary. Climate data yields a strong seasonal structure in NDVI over Africa. iMSM captures the global scales, while iSDS reveals region-specific regime structures. Although interpretability of the latent variables is complex in climate time series, our regime identifiability results still enable interpretable regime-conditioned analyses. Extending iSDS with recent identifiable spatiotemporal frameworks could further disentangle regional effects and improve latent space interpretability (Wang et al., 2025).

Although interpretability of the latent variables is complex in climate time series, our regime identifiability results still enable interpretable regime-conditioned analyses. Extending iSDS with recent identifiable spatiotemporal frameworks could further disentangle regional effects and improve latent space interpretability (Wang et al., 2025).

texts.text: Identifiability of Regime-Switching Models

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 8 Conclusions
texts.text: 8 Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We developed a unified identifiability framework for regime-switching models with multi-lag dependencies. For Markov switching models, we framed the model as a temporal finite mixture and proved identifiability of both the number of regimes and the regime-specific multi-lag transitions in a nonlinear Gaussian setting, with a thorough nonparametric analysis provided in Appendix A. For switching dynamical systems, we established identifiability of latent variables up to permutation and scaling and derived conditions under which regime-dependent latent causal graphs are identifiable up to regime and node permutations. Importantly, the guarantees hold in a fully unsupervised setting via flexible architectural (function class) and noise assumptions. Empirically, we validated the theory on synthetic data and demonstrated iMSM and iSDS across scientific domains, showing that identifiability yields trustworthy interpretations by ensuring unique representations up to acceptable equivalences.
texts.text: We developed a unified identifiability framework for regime-switching models with multi-lag dependencies. For Markov switching models, we framed the model as a temporal finite mixture and proved identifiability of both the number of regimes and the regime-specific multi-lag transitions in a nonlinear Gaussian setting, with a thorough nonparametric analysis provided in Appendix A. For switching dynamical systems, we established identifiability of latent variables up to permutation and scaling and derived conditions under which regime-dependent latent causal graphs are identifiable up to regime and node permutations. Importantly, the guarantees hold in a fully unsupervised setting via flexible architectural (function class) and noise assumptions. Empirically, we validated the theory on synthetic data and demonstrated iMSM and iSDS across scientific domains, showing that identifiability yields trustworthy interpretations by ensuring unique representations up to acceptable equivalences.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: While we provide strong identifiability results for iMSM, our iSDS guarantees assume piecewise linear generative functions and specific noise and function classes, which may not hold universally. On the estimation side, we rely on variational inference and do not prove statistical consistency. Consistency results exist in related literature assuming the variational family has universal approximation properties (Khemakhem et al., 2020). Empirically, we observe strong synthetic performance, with recovery of regime-dependent causal structures becoming more challenging as dimensionality increases.
texts.text: While we provide strong identifiability results for iMSM, our iSDS guarantees assume piecewise linear generative functions and specific noise and function classes, which may not hold universally. On the estimation side, we rely on variational inference and do not prove statistical consistency. Consistency results exist in related literature assuming the variational family has universal approximation properties (Khemakhem et al., 2020). Empirically, we observe strong synthetic performance, with recovery of regime-dependent causal structures becoming more challenging as dimensionality increases.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our framework is complementary to existing methodologies and suggests several directions for future work. Some examples include incorporating contemporaneous effects, continuoustime dynamics, and infinite mixture processes, as well as going beyond piecewise linearity. These extensions would broaden the applicability of identifiable regime-switching models for scientific discovery. Furthermore, our identifiability results extend beyond standard MSMs with time-homogeneous Markov switching to models with time-dependent regime distributions, offering room for experimental exploration.
texts.text: Our framework is complementary to existing methodologies and suggests several directions for future work. Some examples include incorporating contemporaneous effects, continuoustime dynamics, and infinite mixture processes, as well as going beyond piecewise linearity. These extensions would broaden the applicability of identifiable regime-switching models for scientific discovery. Furthermore, our identifiability results extend beyond standard MSMs with time-homogeneous Markov switching to models with time-dependent regime distributions, offering room for experimental exploration.

texts.text: Appendix A. Proof of Theorem 8

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Sketch of the proof: We organise the proof strategy into 4 steps.
texts.text: Sketch of the proof: We organise the proof strategy into 4 steps.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. We show the requirement for identifiability is linear independence of the trajectory family (Appendix A.1).
texts.text: We show the requirement for identifiability is linear independence of the trajectory family (Appendix A.1).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. We provide linear independence results for products of nonparametric functions. We first start with M = 1 (Appendix A.2.1), and we then strengthen the assumptions (Appendix A.2.2) to allow products of M functions (Appendix A.2.3).
texts.text: We provide linear independence results for products of nonparametric functions. We first start with M = 1 (Appendix A.2.1), and we then strengthen the assumptions (Appendix A.2.2) to allow products of M functions (Appendix A.2.3).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. We prove linear independence of the trajectory family for nonparametric transitions of order M using induction (Appendix A.2.4).
texts.text: We prove linear independence of the trajectory family for nonparametric transitions of order M using induction (Appendix A.2.4).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 4. We show the parametric assumptions (m1), (m2) satisfy linear independence of the trajectory family (Appendix A.3).
texts.text: We show the parametric assumptions (m1), (m2) satisfy linear independence of the trajectory family (Appendix A.3).

texts.orig: A.2.1 Preliminaries
texts.text: A.2.1 Preliminaries

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We recall the central result for the case M = 1 from Balsells-Rodas et al. (2024), which we name L inear I ndependence on P roduct functions with 1 consecutive O verlap (LIPO-1). It provides conditions under which linear independence is preserved for products with a single overlapping variable.
texts.text: We recall the central result for the case M = 1 from Balsells-Rodas et al. (2024), which we name L inear I ndependence on P roduct functions with 1 consecutive O verlap (LIPO-1). It provides conditions under which linear independence is preserved for products with a single overlapping variable.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lemma 16 (LIPO-1) Consider two scalar real-valued function families U I := { u i ( y , x ) | i ∈ I } and V J := { v j ( z , y ) | j ∈ J } with x ∈ X , y ∈ R d y and z ∈ R d z . We further make the following assumptions:
texts.text: Lemma 16 (LIPO-1) Consider two scalar real-valued function families U I := { u i ( y , x ) | i ∈ I } and V J := { v j ( z , y ) | j ∈ J } with x ∈ X , y ∈ R d y and z ∈ R d z . We further make the following assumptions:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (a1) Positive function values: u i ( y , x ) > 0 for all i ∈ I, ( y , x ) ∈ R d y ×X . Similar positive function values assumption applies to V J : v j ( z , y ) > 0 for all j ∈ J, ( z , y ) ∈ R d z × R d y .
texts.text: (a1) Positive function values: u i ( y , x ) > 0 for all i ∈ I, ( y , x ) ∈ R d y ×X . Similar positive function values assumption applies to V J : v j ( z , y ) > 0 for all j ∈ J, ( z , y ) ∈ R d z × R d y .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (a2) Unique indexing: for U I , i = i ′ ∈ I ⇔∃ x , y s.t. u i ( y , x ) = u i ′ ( y , x ) . Similar unique indexing assumption applies to V J ;
texts.text: (a2) Unique indexing: for U I , i = i ′ ∈ I ⇔∃ x , y s.t. u i ( y , x ) = u i ′ ( y , x ) . Similar unique indexing assumption applies to V J ;

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (a3) Linear independence under finite mixtures on specific nonzero measure subsets for U I : for any nonzero measure subset Y ⊂ R d y , U I contains linearly independent functions under finite mixtures on ( y , x ) ∈ Y × X .
texts.text: (a3) Linear independence under finite mixtures on specific nonzero measure subsets for U I : for any nonzero measure subset Y ⊂ R d y , U I contains linearly independent functions under finite mixtures on ( y , x ) ∈ Y × X .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (a4) Linear independence under finite mixtures on specific nonzero measure subsets for V J : there exists a nonzero measure subset Y ⊂ R d y , such that for any nonzero measure subsets Y ′ ⊂ Y and Z ⊂ R d z , V J contains linearly independent functions under finite mixtures on ( z , y ) ∈ Z × Y ′ ;
texts.text: (a4) Linear independence under finite mixtures on specific nonzero measure subsets for V J : there exists a nonzero measure subset Y ⊂ R d y , such that for any nonzero measure subsets Y ′ ⊂ Y and Z ⊂ R d z , V J contains linearly independent functions under finite mixtures on ( z , y ) ∈ Z × Y ′ ;

texts.orig: Notation. In the main text and above, we write the coordinate variables in ascending order. However, for better readability, in this section we write variables in descending order, z M , . . . , z 1 and y M , . . . y 1 , to mirror the product family construction. We also use the following letters to denote distinct notions in our proofs: r denotes the number of consecutive products, ℓ denotes the product factor index, and q denotes the class label in the split (used later in LIPO-M, Lemma 21). We also remove d y , d z (used in LIPO-1), and instead we use d to denote the dimensionality of a single variable y i , i ∈ [ M ] or z .
texts.text: Notation. In the main text and above, we write the coordinate variables in ascending order. However, for better readability, in this section we write variables in descending order, z M , . . . , z 1 and y M , . . . y 1 , to mirror the product family construction. We also use the following letters to denote distinct notions in our proofs: r denotes the number of consecutive products, ℓ denotes the product factor index, and q denotes the class label in the split (used later in LIPO-M, Lemma 21). We also remove d y , d z (used in LIPO-1), and instead we use d to denote the dimensionality of a single variable y i , i ∈ [ M ] or z .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Below we explore assumptions on nonparametric families with M +1 variables (aligned with some transition distribution p ( z t | z t -1 , . . . , z t -M , s t ) , such that the product of M consecutive variables satisfies (a1), (a2), and (a4-a6). This would allow us to use LIPO-1 (or an equivalent extension) to prove linear independence under finite mixtures of the trajectory family P T,M A , B . We note assumption (a2) is redundant, as it holds for U I and V J if (a3) and (a4) hold, respectively. Therefore, we remove (a2) from our theoretical analysis for simplicity. Given a family V J = { v j ( z , y M , . . . , y 1 ) | j ∈ J } with variables ( z , y M , . . . , y 1 ) defined on × M +1 R d , we provide the assumption modifications following the enumeration presented in LIPO-1 (Lemma 16). We keep (a3) unchanged as we will not modify the family U I .

texts.orig: Contrary to the modifications of (a1) and (a6), we note that the extension of (a4) and (a5) to M consecutive products requires them to be strengthened respectively to (b4) and (b5). For (b4), the nonzero measure subset Y must now have full measure. This differs from the case M = 1 where Y can have any nonzero measure, and the intuition behind this requirement can be explained from the appearance of conditioned-conditioned overlaps for M > 1 (See Eq. (34)). For (b5), linear dependence only implied by repeating functions for fixed y is strengthened, and now, this must be satisfied for repeating functions for certain fixed subgroups of variables from y M , . . . , y 1 .

For (b5), linear dependence only implied by repeating functions for fixed y is strengthened, and now, this must be satisfied for repeating functions for certain fixed subgroups of variables from y M , . . . , y 1 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Given that assumption (b5) does not extend similarly as (b4), we generalise LIPO-1 to LIPOM (Linear Independence on Product functions with M consecutive Overlapping variables) using Lemma 20 to show linear independence under finite mixtures when V J is defined as a product of M functions with consecutive variables.
texts.text: Given that assumption (b5) does not extend similarly as (b4), we generalise LIPO-1 to LIPOM (Linear Independence on Product functions with M consecutive Overlapping variables) using Lemma 20 to show linear independence under finite mixtures when V J is defined as a product of M functions with consecutive variables.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For convenience, we denote the elements of J as indices despite being a tuple. Note that the choices of S 0 and γ ij are independent of any ( x , y , z ) values, but might be dependent on Z . We wish to follow the logic presented in LIPO-1 (Lemma 16) under (a1-a6). Below we establish some connections. First, we know that the family V J as defined in (b) is a product of r elements with 1 ≤ r ≤ M , all of which satisfy (b1) and (b4-b5). Lemma 19, shows that there exists some full-measure Y , such that for any subset Z ×Y ′ ⊂ R d z ×Y , the family V J is linearly independent under finite mixtures. This is stronger than (a4) in Lemma 16 because (b4) provides a full-measure Y , while (a4) only requires a nonzero measure Y . Furthermore, it also satisfies (a1) as it is defined as a product of r functions that satisfy (b1), with 1 ≤ r ≤ M . Therefore, we follow the same arguments as in Lemma 16, which show that the index set S 0 contains at least 2 different indices ( i, j ) and ( i, j ′ ) with j = j ′ . Moreover, we define J 0 = { j ∈ J |∃ ( i, j ) ∈ S 0 } the set of all possible j indices that appear in S 0 , where | J 0 | < + ∞ and the following set Y 0 := { β ∈ R d y | ∑ i :( i,j ) ∈ S 0 γ ij u i ( y = β , x ) = 0 , ∀ x ∈ X} can only have zero measure in R d y from (a3). Again following Lemma 16, we have a nonzero measure set Y ′ := Y\Y 0 ⊂ Y where we choose Y from the full-measure set in (b4). Then, we have for each β ∈ Y ′ , there exists x ∈ X such that ∑ i :( i,j ) ∈ S 0 γ ij u i ( y = β , x ) = 0 for at least two j indices in J 0 . This implies for each β ∈ Y ′ , { v j ( z , y = β ) | j ∈ J 0 } contains linearly dependent functions on z ∈ Z .
texts.text: For convenience, we denote the elements of J as indices despite being a tuple. Note that the choices of S 0 and γ ij are independent of any ( x , y , z ) values, but might be dependent on Z . We wish to follow the logic presented in LIPO-1 (Lemma 16) under (a1-a6). Below we establish some connections. First, we know that the family V J as defined in (b) is a product of r elements with 1 ≤ r ≤ M , all of which satisfy (b1) and (b4-b5). Lemma 19, shows that there exists some full-measure Y , such that for any subset Z ×Y ′ ⊂ R d z ×Y , the family V J is linearly independent under finite mixtures. This is stronger than (a4) in Lemma 16 because (b4) provides a full-measure Y , while (a4) only requires a nonzero measure Y . Furthermore, it also satisfies (a1) as it is defined as a product of r functions that satisfy (b1), with 1 ≤ r ≤ M . Therefore, we follow the same arguments as in Lemma 16, which show that the index set S 0 contains at least 2 different indices ( i, j ) and ( i, j ′ ) with j = j ′ . Moreover, we define J 0 = { j ∈ J |∃ ( i, j ) ∈ S 0 } the set of all possible j indices that appear in S 0 , where | J 0 | < + ∞ and the following set Y 0 := { β ∈ R d y | ∑ i :( i,j ) ∈ S 0 γ ij u i ( y = β , x ) = 0 , ∀ x ∈ X} can only have zero measure in R d y from (a3). Again following Lemma 16, we have a nonzero measure set Y ′ := Y\Y 0 ⊂ Y where we choose Y from the full-measure set in (b4). Then, we have for each β ∈ Y ′ , there exists x ∈ X such that ∑ i :( i,j ) ∈ S 0 γ ij u i ( y = β , x ) = 0 for at least two j indices in J 0 . This implies for each β ∈ Y ′ , { v j ( z , y = β ) | j ∈ J 0 } contains linearly dependent functions on z ∈ Z .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We will now write J , S 0 and J 0 tuple format, as we need to expand the product family V J . texts.orig: Proof We prove the statement by contradiction. Define V J := G B , z := z t , and ( y 1 , . . . , y M ) := ( z t -1 , . . . , z t -M ) from (b4); and w.l.o.g. set Y := R mM . If (b4) does not hold, then there exist nonzero measure sets Y ′ ⊂ Y and Z ⊂ R m such that G B contains linearly dependent functions. From Proposition 23, this means there exists an index set B 0 ⊂ B with |B 0 | < + ∞ such that the unique indexing assumption (Eq. (15)) does not hold in Y ′ . From the intersection of moments assumption, we know that this set is Y ′ = ⋃ b = b ′ ∈B 0 X b,b ′ , which has zero measure. This contradicts the requirement for a nonzero measure set Y ′ ⊂ Y in (b4).
texts.text: Proof We prove the statement by contradiction. Define V J := G B , z := z t , and ( y 1 , . . . , y M ) := ( z t -1 , . . . , z t -M ) from (b4); and w.l.o.g. set Y := R mM . If (b4) does not hold, then there exist nonzero measure sets Y ′ ⊂ Y and Z ⊂ R m such that G B contains linearly dependent functions. From Proposition 23, this means there exists an index set B 0 ⊂ B with |B 0 | < + ∞ such that the unique indexing assumption (Eq. (15)) does not hold in Y ′ . From the intersection of moments assumption, we know that this set is Y ′ = ⋃ b = b ′ ∈B 0 X b,b ′ , which has zero measure. This contradicts the requirement for a nonzero measure set Y ′ ⊂ Y in (b4).

texts.orig: Proof We first clarify the alignment from assumptions (m1-m2) to (d1-d3) in Theorem 27. (d1) and (m1) are equivalent. (d2) is satisfied from (m2) as analytic functions are C ∞ , and therefore continuous. From Mityagin (2015), we know the zero set of an analytic function is measure zero. Therefore, (m2) implies that the intersections between on means or covariances for any b, b ′ ∈ B , with b = b ′ are also measure zero, which implies (d3). Therefore, from Theorem 27, the trajectory family under the Gaussian transition family G B is linearly independent under finite mixtures. By Theorem 15, linear independence under finite mixtures of the trajectory family implies identifiability of the MSM in terms of Def. 7.
texts.text: Proof We first clarify the alignment from assumptions (m1-m2) to (d1-d3) in Theorem 27. (d1) and (m1) are equivalent. (d2) is satisfied from (m2) as analytic functions are C ∞ , and therefore continuous. From Mityagin (2015), we know the zero set of an analytic function is measure zero. Therefore, (m2) implies that the intersections between on means or covariances for any b, b ′ ∈ B , with b = b ′ are also measure zero, which implies (d3). Therefore, from Theorem 27, the trajectory family under the Gaussian transition family G B is linearly independent under finite mixtures. By Theorem 15, linear independence under finite mixtures of the trajectory family implies identifiability of the MSM in terms of Def. 7.

texts.orig: Appendix B. Proof of SDS identifiability
texts.text: Appendix B. Proof of SDS identifiability

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We extend the results from Kivva et al. (2022) and Balsells-Rodas et al. (2024) with our multi-lag MSM family as a prior distribution for z 1: T . The strategy requires finding some open set where the transformations F and G from two identically distributed SDSs are invertible. Then, identifiability can be shown thanks to piecewise linearity of F and G , and the multi-lag MSM being an analytic distribution and closed under affine transformations.
texts.text: We extend the results from Kivva et al. (2022) and Balsells-Rodas et al. (2024) with our multi-lag MSM family as a prior distribution for z 1: T . The strategy requires finding some open set where the transformations F and G from two identically distributed SDSs are invertible. Then, identifiability can be shown thanks to piecewise linearity of F and G , and the multi-lag MSM being an analytic distribution and closed under affine transformations.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorem 34 (adapted from Balsells-Rodas et al. (2024)) Let F , G : R Tm → R Tn be factored piecewise affine mappings, with x t := f ( z t ) and x ′ t := g ( z ′ t ) , for 1 ≤ t ≤ T . Let z 1: T and z ′ 1: T be distributed according to the identifiable multi-lag MSM family M T,M NL under assumptions (m1-m2). Assume F ( z 1: T ) and G ( z ′ 1: T ) are identically distributed, and that there exists x (0) 1: T ∈ R Tn and δ > 0 such that F and G are invertible on B ( x (0) 1: T , δ ) ∩ F ( R Tm ) . Then there exists an invertible factored affine transformation H such that H ( z 1: T ) = z ′ 1: T .
texts.text: Theorem 34 (adapted from Balsells-Rodas et al. (2024)) Let F , G : R Tm → R Tn be factored piecewise affine mappings, with x t := f ( z t ) and x ′ t := g ( z ′ t ) , for 1 ≤ t ≤ T . Let z 1: T and z ′ 1: T be distributed according to the identifiable multi-lag MSM family M T,M NL under assumptions (m1-m2). Assume F ( z 1: T ) and G ( z ′ 1: T ) are identically distributed, and that there exists x (0) 1: T ∈ R Tn and δ > 0 such that F and G are invertible on B ( x (0) 1: T , δ ) ∩ F ( R Tm ) . Then there exists an invertible factored affine transformation H such that H ( z 1: T ) = z ′ 1: T .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof Since a multi-lag MSM is closed under affine transformations (Balsells-Rodas et al., 2024, Prop C.2), and the distributions of the multi-lag MSM family M T,M NL under assumptions (m1-m2) are analytic functions, the proof is identical to Balsells-Rodas et al. (2024).
texts.text: Proof Since a multi-lag MSM is closed under affine transformations (Balsells-Rodas et al., 2024, Prop C.2), and the distributions of the multi-lag MSM family M T,M NL under assumptions (m1-m2) are analytic functions, the proof is identical to Balsells-Rodas et al. (2024).

texts.orig: Proof It follows from Balsells-Rodas et al. (2024). Assume there exists another model that generates the same distribution from Eq.(21), with a prior p ′ ∈ M T,M NL under assumptions (m1-m2), and nonlinear emission F ′ , composed by f ′ which is weakly injective and piecewise linear: i.e., ( F # p )( x 1: T ) = ( F ′ # p ′ )( x 1: T ) .
texts.text: Proof It follows from Balsells-Rodas et al. (2024). Assume there exists another model that generates the same distribution from Eq.(21), with a prior p ′ ∈ M T,M NL under assumptions (m1-m2), and nonlinear emission F ′ , composed by f ′ which is weakly injective and piecewise linear: i.e., ( F # p )( x 1: T ) = ( F ′ # p ′ )( x 1: T ) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: From weakly-injectivity, at least for some x 0 ∈ R n and δ > 0 , f is invertible on B ( x 0 , 2 δ ) ∩ f ( R m ) . This satisfies the preconditions from Corollary 33, which implies there exists x (1) 1: T ∈ B ( x (0) 1: T , δ ) and δ 1 > 0 such that both F and F ′ are invertible on B ( x (1) 1: T , δ 1 ) ∩ F ( R Tm ) . Thus, by Theorem 34, there exists an affine transformation H such that H ( z 1: T ) = z ′ 1: T , i.e., p ∈ M T,M NL is identifiable up to affine transformations.
texts.text: From weakly-injectivity, at least for some x 0 ∈ R n and δ > 0 , f is invertible on B ( x 0 , 2 δ ) ∩ f ( R m ) . This satisfies the preconditions from Corollary 33, which implies there exists x (1) 1: T ∈ B ( x (0) 1: T , δ ) and δ 1 > 0 such that both F and F ′ are invertible on B ( x (1) 1: T , δ 1 ) ∩ F ( R Tm ) . Thus, by Theorem 34, there exists an affine transformation H such that H ( z 1: T ) = z ′ 1: T , i.e., p ∈ M T,M NL is identifiable up to affine transformations.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof It follows from Theorem 12 (i) and Theorem E.1 from Kivva et al. (2022), which is applicable for any t ∈ { M +1 , . . . , T } as z t | z t -1 , . . . z t -M is a GMM. Note that t ∈ { 1 , . . . , M } is already covered by Kivva et al. (2022) as it reduces to the initial distribution case. From Theorem 12 (i) we have identifiability up to affine transformations: i.e., we have some z ′ 1: T such that z ′ t = A z t + b , t ∈ { 1 , . . . , T } . Given (s1) and (s2), for any t ∈ { M +1 , . . . , T } , the conditions of Theorem E.1 in Kivva et al. (2022) are satisfied in a nonzero measure set in the space of ( z t -1 , . . . z t -M ) . Therefore, we can find ˜ A such that ˜ z t = ˜ A -1 z ′ t = ˜ P ˜ D z t + ˜ b , where ˜ P and ˜ D are permutation and scaling matrices respectively.
texts.text: Proof It follows from Theorem 12 (i) and Theorem E.1 from Kivva et al. (2022), which is applicable for any t ∈ { M +1 , . . . , T } as z t | z t -1 , . . . z t -M is a GMM. Note that t ∈ { 1 , . . . , M } is already covered by Kivva et al. (2022) as it reduces to the initial distribution case. From Theorem 12 (i) we have identifiability up to affine transformations: i.e., we have some z ′ 1: T such that z ′ t = A z t + b , t ∈ { 1 , . . . , T } . Given (s1) and (s2), for any t ∈ { M +1 , . . . , T } , the conditions of Theorem E.1 in Kivva et al. (2022) are satisfied in a nonzero measure set in the space of ( z t -1 , . . . z t -M ) . Therefore, we can find ˜ A such that ˜ z t = ˜ A -1 z ′ t = ˜ P ˜ D z t + ˜ b , where ˜ P and ˜ D are permutation and scaling matrices respectively.

texts.text: We provide additional details of the data generation process. The initial Gaussian components are sampled from N (0 , 0 . 7 2 I ) . For the mean transition functions m ( · , k ) , we already indicate the use of locally connected networks (Zheng et al., 2018) where network dependencies follow a regime-dependent causal structure. The networks consist of two-layer MLPs with 16 hidden units and cosine activations. The covariance matrix Σ ( · , k ) is designed as follows depending on the setting:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Constant noise: fixed, Σ = 0 . 01 I .
texts.text: Constant noise: fixed, Σ = 0 . 01 I .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Heterogeneous noise: Σ ( k ) = diag ( σ 2 k ) , where for each k ∈ [ K ] and i ∈ [ m ] , σ 2 k,i ∼ U (0 . 005 , 0 . 08) , such that assumption (s2) holds. That is, we check whether there are at least two regimes k 1 , k 2 ∈ [ K ] with distinct diagonal ratios across dimensions, and re-sample otherwise.
texts.text: Heterogeneous noise: Σ ( k ) = diag ( σ 2 k ) , where for each k ∈ [ K ] and i ∈ [ m ] , σ 2 k,i ∼ U (0 . 005 , 0 . 08) , such that assumption (s2) holds. That is, we check whether there are at least two regimes k 1 , k 2 ∈ [ K ] with distinct diagonal ratios across dimensions, and re-sample otherwise.

texts.text: History-dependent noise: Σ ( · , k ) = diag ( C k · sigmoid( m ( · , k ))) , where C k ∼ U (0 . 05 , 0 . 1) .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: E.2 Training Specifications
texts.text: E.2 Training Specifications

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The experiments are implemented in PyTorch (Paszke et al., 2019) and performed on NVIDIA RTX 2080 Ti GPUs, except for the experiments on climate data with SDSs, where we used NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090 GPUs. In all our experiments, we use Adam optimiser (Kingma and Ba, 2015) with a learning rate of 7 · 10 -3 for iMSM, and 5 · 10 -4 for iSDS. Other specific configurations are dependent on the dataset and indicated below. Similar to related approaches (Hälvä and Hyvarinen, 2020), we use random restarts to achieve better parameter estimates: 3 for iMSM initialisation (see below), and 5 for the full models.
texts.text: The experiments are implemented in PyTorch (Paszke et al., 2019) and performed on NVIDIA RTX 2080 Ti GPUs, except for the experiments on climate data with SDSs, where we used NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090 GPUs. In all our experiments, we use Adam optimiser (Kingma and Ba, 2015) with a learning rate of 7 · 10 -3 for iMSM, and 5 · 10 -4 for iSDS. Other specific configurations are dependent on the dataset and indicated below. Similar to related approaches (Hälvä and Hyvarinen, 2020), we use random restarts to achieve better parameter estimates: 3 for iMSM initialisation (see below), and 5 for the full models.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To train iSDS, we generally follow a 4-stage procedure.
texts.text: To train iSDS, we generally follow a 4-stage procedure.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Initialisation (iMSM): We initialise the latent dynamical prior by training iMSM on the observations after dimensionality reduction via PCA. To train iMSM, we use a batch size of 100 , set learning rate 7 · 10 -3 , and decrease it by a factor of 0.5 when the likelihood plateaus, up to twice. We use 3 random restarts, and select the best model on training likelihood to initialise iSDS.
texts.text: Initialisation (iMSM): We initialise the latent dynamical prior by training iMSM on the observations after dimensionality reduction via PCA. To train iMSM, we use a batch size of 100 , set learning rate 7 · 10 -3 , and decrease it by a factor of 0.5 when the likelihood plateaus, up to twice. We use 3 random restarts, and select the best model on training likelihood to initialise iSDS.

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: Balsells-Rodas, Matsui, Mediano, Wang and Li
texts.text: Balsells-Rodas, Matsui, Mediano, Wang and Li

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Encoder-decoder pre-training: We find an initialisation for the encoder and decoder by freezing the gradients on the dynamical prior.
texts.text: Encoder-decoder pre-training: We find an initialisation for the encoder and decoder by freezing the gradients on the dynamical prior.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Warmup phase: iSDS is trained jointly but we keep the switch parameters π, Q frozen. This helps us prevent state collapse.
texts.text: Warmup phase: iSDS is trained jointly but we keep the switch parameters π, Q frozen. This helps us prevent state collapse.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Final phase: All the networks are fine-tuned.
texts.text: Final phase: All the networks are fine-tuned.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Related works (Dong et al., 2020; Ansari et al., 2021) introduce annealing to force uniform regime posterior assignments and ensure the model uses all the switches for inference. Instead, we find that combining initialisation from iMSM and random restarts prevents state collapse. Below we describe the specific configurations and network architecture choices for each dataset.
texts.text: Related works (Dong et al., 2020; Ansari et al., 2021) introduce annealing to force uniform regime posterior assignments and ensure the model uses all the switches for inference. Instead, we find that combining initialisation from iMSM and random restarts prevents state collapse. Below we describe the specific configurations and network architecture choices for each dataset.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Synthetic data. For training, we use a batch size of 100, initialise the latent iMSM as described above, pre-train the encoder and decoder for 40 epochs, and warmup for 10 additional epochs. We then unfreeze all the gradients and train for 700 epochs with a learning rate of 5 · 10 -4 and decay of 0.8 every 200 epochs. The transition means and covariances follow the architecture from the data generation process. The encoder and decoder are two-layer MLPs with 128 hidden units and Leaky ReLU activations. We regularise the Jacobian transitions with η = 0 . 05 in the loss function Eq. (30).
texts.text: Synthetic data. For training, we use a batch size of 100, initialise the latent iMSM as described above, pre-train the encoder and decoder for 40 epochs, and warmup for 10 additional epochs. We then unfreeze all the gradients and train for 700 epochs with a learning rate of 5 · 10 -4 and decay of 0.8 every 200 epochs. The transition means and covariances follow the architecture from the data generation process. The encoder and decoder are two-layer MLPs with 128 hidden units and Leaky ReLU activations. We regularise the Jacobian transitions with η = 0 . 05 in the loss function Eq. (30).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Brain activity data. Training follows the initialisation phase of iSDS, and the transitions consist of two-layer MLPs with 32 hidden units with GELU activations.
texts.text: Brain activity data. Training follows the initialisation phase of iSDS, and the transitions consist of two-layer MLPs with 32 hidden units with GELU activations.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Financial data. Training largely follows the synthetic data procedure with minor modifications, and we use the same setting for log prices and log returns. In the initialisation phase, we use the whole time series to encourage better regime learning, with a learning rate of 7 · 10 -3 which is reduced by a factor of 0.5 if likelihood plateaus for 10 iterations. iMSM training stops after the model plateaus 3 times. The rest of the phases follow the procedure described in the synthetic data with subsampled time series of length T = 200 . We train for 100 k iterations, with a learning rate of 5 · 10 -4 and a decay of 0.8 every 40 k iterations. Pre-train and warmup phases last for 2 k and 8 k iterations respectively. The architecture consists of encoder-decoder from two-layer MLPs with 256 hidden dimensions and Leaky ReLU activations, and mean transitions of two-layer MLPs with 32 hidden dimensions and GELU activations, and regime-dependent diagonal covariances. Here we consider η = 0 in the loss function Eq. (30).
texts.text: Financial data. Training largely follows the synthetic data procedure with minor modifications, and we use the same setting for log prices and log returns. In the initialisation phase, we use the whole time series to encourage better regime learning, with a learning rate of 7 · 10 -3 which is reduced by a factor of 0.5 if likelihood plateaus for 10 iterations. iMSM training stops after the model plateaus 3 times. The rest of the phases follow the procedure described in the synthetic data with subsampled time series of length T = 200 . We train for 100 k iterations, with a learning rate of 5 · 10 -4 and a decay of 0.8 every 40 k iterations. Pre-train and warmup phases last for 2 k and 8 k iterations respectively. The architecture consists of encoder-decoder from two-layer MLPs with 256 hidden dimensions and Leaky ReLU activations, and mean transitions of two-layer MLPs with 32 hidden dimensions and GELU activations, and regime-dependent diagonal covariances. Here we consider η = 0 in the loss function Eq. (30).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Climate data. Training follows the financial time series, where initialisation trains iMSM on the whole time series with T = 360 time steps. We later use the iMSM trained for the interpretability analysis presented in Section 7.3. To train the rest of iSDS, we subsample sequences of length T = 100 . We pre-train the encoder-decoder for 250 k iterations, with a learning rate of 5 · 10 -4 and a decay of 0.8 every 100 k iterations. We then warmup iSDS for 4000 iterations, and we run the final phase for 120 k iterations, with a decay of 0.8 every 20 k iterations. For the network architecture, the transitions are the same as used in financial time series, and we use CNNs for inference and generation. For inference, we use a 9 -layer CNN with increasing channels, kernel size 3 , and padding 1 , Leaky ReLU activations, and we alternate between using stride 2 and 1 . We start with 64 channels and double the channels every two layers, until we have 512 channels. We then run a three-layer MLP with Leaky ReLU activations and 256 hidden dimensions. For generation, we use a similar network, starting with a two-layer MLP with Leaky ReLU activations and 256 hidden dimensions, and use transposed convolutions instead of convolutions (with the same configuration as before). We also consider η = 0 in this setting.
texts.text: Climate data. Training follows the financial time series, where initialisation trains iMSM on the whole time series with T = 360 time steps. We later use the iMSM trained for the interpretability analysis presented in Section 7.3. To train the rest of iSDS, we subsample sequences of length T = 100 . We pre-train the encoder-decoder for 250 k iterations, with a learning rate of 5 · 10 -4 and a decay of 0.8 every 100 k iterations. We then warmup iSDS for 4000 iterations, and we run the final phase for 120 k iterations, with a decay of 0.8 every 20 k iterations. For the network architecture, the transitions are the same as used in financial time series, and we use CNNs for inference and generation. For inference, we use a 9 -layer CNN with increasing channels, kernel size 3 , and padding 1 , Leaky ReLU activations, and we alternate between using stride 2 and 1 . We start with 64 channels and double the channels every two layers, until we have 512 channels. We then run a three-layer MLP with Leaky ReLU activations and 256 hidden dimensions. For generation, we use a similar network, starting with a two-layer MLP with Leaky ReLU activations and 256 hidden dimensions, and use transposed convolutions instead of convolutions (with the same configuration as before). We also consider η = 0 in this setting.

texts.text: where we use τ = 0 . 05 in our experiments, and 1 ( · ) denotes the indicator function, which equals 1 if the argument is true and 0 otherwise. For lag M , ˆ G k has shape m × ( mM ) (rows: current variables; columns: lagged variables across all M lags). Regimes are aligned by the permutation that maximises the regime F 1 score (as above). For latent alignment, we decompose the affine map fitted for the L 2 distance as A ≈ DP , with P a permutation matrix and D diagonal. P is obtained by selecting maximal-absolute entries per row. For each lag τ ∈ [ M ] with ˆ E k,τ denoting the lagτ adjacency matrix of ˆ G k , we permute with P ˆ E σ ( k ) ,τ P T for latent variable alignment. Finally, we report the average F 1 score between the aligned estimate and the ground-truth graphs G 1: K , averaged over regimes.

texts.orig: Figure 18 reports test log-likelihoods for the Awake and Anaesthetised conditions with increasing regimes K . Performance improves with larger K and plateaus around K ≈ 10 . We adopt an overcomplete setting with K = 15 in the main experiments, and expect the models to use a subset the regimes in practice. As reported in Table 1 (Section 7.1), the total regime usage is 53 . 33% for Awake and 33 . 33% for Anaesthetised , corresponding to 8 and 5 active regimes. Allowing a larger candidate set of regimes helps prevent state collapse, as unused regimes remain inactive, and test likelihood does not degrade.

texts.orig: We also examine latent transition structure across regimes for log returns (Fig. 19), and the edge-intensity distribution (Fig. 19d). The histogram is computed using the average absolute off-diagonal Jacobian entries for each regime, and it is concentrated near zero. This indicates weak latent interactions. For interpretability, we show the higher-intensity subset (right of the red threshold). In Regimes 0 and 1, z 0 and z 1 dominate the latent dynamics (consistent with global market factors). Furthermore, the high-volatility regime (Regime 0), exhibits denser interactions than the medium and low-volatility regimes (Regime 2), consistent with the need for stronger dynamical adjustments during turbulent periods.
texts.text: We also examine latent transition structure across regimes for log returns (Fig. 19), and the edge-intensity distribution (Fig. 19d). The histogram is computed using the average absolute off-diagonal Jacobian entries for each regime, and it is concentrated near zero. This indicates weak latent interactions. texts.label: text
texts.orig: For log price data, Figure 20 shows regime-specific latent transition graphs and the corresponding edge-intensity histogram (Fig. 20c). The edge distribution is centred near 0 . 1 with a long tail of stronger interactions. The latent dynamics differ across regimes, with the lateral/bear regime (Regime 0) exhibiting simpler and weaker dynamics compared to the bull regime (Regime 1). The two global factors ( z 2 and z 1 0 ) are involved differently across regimes. Notably, interactions are concentrated on z 5 and z 8 , which are aligned with Consumer Staples, Real Estate, and Materials. This suggests these sectors require relatively complex, regime-dependent adjustments (e.g., Consumer Staples as a defensive sector; Real Estate as rate-sensitive).

texts.orig: Model selection. The data settings differ between iMSM and iSDS. For iMSM, we follow Varando et al. (2022) and project land pixels onto 10 principal components. For iSDS, we train on random 64 × 64 land patches with subsequences of length T = 100 . We fix K = 4 regimes and vary the number of lags M . Figure 21 reports held-out performance as a function of M : test log-likelihood and ELBO (higher is better in both cases). To make scales comparable, we report both metrics per observation (i.e., per PC for iMSM and per pixel for iSDS). We observe that iMSM performs best at M = 1 , while iSDS benefits from higher orders, with M = 2 performing best. We note that iMSM operates on globally mixed

texts.orig: Regime calendars. In Section 7.3, Tables 2 and 3 report ENSO correlations by season for iMSM and iSDS respectively. We visualise the within-year regime occupancy of iMSM in Figure 22. As mentioned, the dynamics are structured yearly within 3 periods: from March to May, from June to September, and from October to February. The structures remain consistent for unobserved years. For iSDS, we observe 5 types of recurrent year-based patterns depending on location. Figures 23-25 show Ethiopia, Sahel, and South Africa. We omit calendars for Sahara and Congo, as they predominantly use a single regime. Across regions, the reported months in Table 3 agree with the visual occupancy, which remain consistent for unseen years.

texts.orig: · iSDS: For each region, we sample 10 patches and sort the latent variables according to the iSDS regime posteriors. Then, for each season we compute similar per-patch correlation matrices of size 20 × 16 (latent variables, lags), and aggregate patches using Fisher z -averaging (Fisher, 1915). Table 3 reports peak correlations and lag (days) for each region and season, and we visualise the regional correlation matrices in Figure 27.

Then, for each season we compute similar per-patch correlation matrices of size 20 × 16 (latent variables, lags), and aggregate patches using Fisher z -averaging (Fisher, 1915). Table 3 reports peak correlations and lag (days) for each region and season, and we visualise the regional correlation matrices in Figure 27.
